VcPass_explanation: The target 'could have been sold' instantiates VcPass: it contains the auxiliary sequence 'have' + 'been' + past participle 'sold' (a transitive verb), the grammatical subject 'ointment' is the semantic patient (entity affected by the selling event), and the agent is omitted, satisfying all criteria for passive voice morphology.

FullText: » It is written that the Son of man will depart . The man who betrays him will experience much grief . It would have been better if that man had not been born . «
ASTrns_explanation: The target 'would have been better' is a copular construction with an adjectival predicate (better) that does not encode a two-participant transitive event; it lacks both an overt direct object and the agent-patient semantic structure required for ASTrns.
ASUncc_explanation: The copular verb 'be' in 'would have been better' does not license an external argument; the sole overt argument functions as the syntactic subject linked to a predicative complement ('better'), matching the copular unaccusative pattern.
ASUnrg_explanation: The target 'would have been better' is a copular construction with 'been' as the auxiliary and 'better' as a predicate adjective, not a lexical verb encoding a one-participant event with an external argument; it expresses a state/condition rather than an agent-initiated activity.
AktAcc_explanation: The target 'would have been better' is a stative predicate expressing a comparative evaluation or state of affairs, not a dynamic event with a durative process directed toward a defined endpoint or result state.
AktAch_explanation: The target 'would have been better' is a stative predicate expressing a comparative quality or condition, not a punctual transition into a new state; it lacks the dynamic change-of-state semantics required for AktAch classification.
AktAct_explanation: The target 'would have been better' is a stative copular construction expressing a comparative evaluation or result state, not a dynamic durative situation involving ongoing action or natural activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'would have been better' is a stative predicate expressing a state or condition, not a dynamic punctual event; it lacks the instantaneous, bounded occurrence quality required for semelfactive classification.
AktState_explanation: The target 'would have been better' denotes a static relational/evaluative state (a comparative quality condition) that holds without dynamic change or progression, matching the stative feature criteria.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'would have been better' occurs in the apodosis (result clause) of a counterfactual conditional ('if that man had not been born'), expressing an unfulfilled, contrary-to-fact situation.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'would have been better' is not negated; the negation ('had not been born') applies to the conditional clause, not to the target gram itself.
DtExp_explanation: The target is a counterfactual conditional expressing a hypothetical state of affairs ('it would have been better'), not an experiential claim about whether an event occurred in the subject's life history.
DtRes_explanation: This is a stative copula construction ('would have been better') that describes a hypothetical state rather than encoding a transition from one state to another; it does not indicate a change of state with a resulting state currently in effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'would have been better' is a counterfactual conditional expressing a hypothetical state contrary to fact, not a situation extending continuously from a past point to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'it would have been better' is a counterfactual conditional expressing a stative evaluation or comparison, not a bounded transition or discrete event; the construction profiles an ongoing or habitual state of affairs rather than a single completed occurrence.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'would have been better' contains the modal auxiliary 'would' combined with 'have been', presenting a counterfactual/hypothetical evaluation of a non-actual situation rather than asserting a realized fact.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'would have been better' contains a modal auxiliary 'would' preceding 'have', which matches the set_to_1_if criterion for modal perfect forms.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'would have been better' is a copular construction with the adjective 'better' as the complement, not a passive voice construction with a past participle of a transitive verb.

FullText: » It is written that the Son of man will depart . The man who betrays him will experience much grief . It would have been better if that man had not been born . «
ASTrns_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('had not been born'), and the lexical verb 'born' licenses both an external argument (the agent/causer of birth, unexpressed) and an internal argument (the patient 'that man'), satisfying the passive condition in set_to_1_if.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'be born' is a change-of-state event where the sole argument (that man) functions as an internal argument (theme/patient) undergoing the event of coming into existence, and no external argument is licensed.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'born' is unaccusative (denoting a coming-into-being event), where the subject 'that man' functions as an internal argument undergoing the event rather than as an external agent/initiator, which disqualifies it from ASUnrg.
AktAcc_explanation: The event 'had not been born' is a punctual boundary transition marking entry into existence, lacking a durative process phase directed toward a defined endpoint.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'be born' denotes a punctual transition into existence/a new state that occurs at a definable temporal boundary, cannot be interrupted mid-process, and naturally accepts time-point modification (e.g., 'born at noon'), satisfying multiple set_to_1_if criteria for achievement aspect.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'be born' denotes a punctual event with a defined result state (existence), not a dynamic durative situation; it lacks the ongoing, homogeneous character required for AktAct.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had not been born' entails a resulting change of state (existence vs. non-existence) and is not a punctual dynamic occurrence without consequent result, thus it does not instantiate AktSemel.
AktState_explanation: The verb phrase 'had not been born' denotes a bounded instantaneous event (birth) with a definite endpoint, not a state that holds over time without dynamic change or progression.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'had not been born' occurs in the protasis of a counterfactual conditional ('if that man had not been born'), expressing an unfulfilled past condition contrary to fact, paired with the apodosis 'It would have been better'.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had not been born' contains the auxiliary 'had' negated with 'not', directly instantiating the ClNegPol feature as the event of being born is presented as not occurring.
DtExp_explanation: This is a negative Perfect ('had not been born') that functions as a claim about the absence of an event in the subject's life history, matching the set_to_1_if criterion for negative Perfects.
DtRes_explanation: The construction 'had not been born' is a stative copula (be + past participle) describing a state rather than encoding a transition; it does not entail that a resulting state persists at the reference time, but rather expresses a counterfactual condition.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'had not been born' expresses a counterfactual state contrary to fact, not a continuous situation extending to the reference time; it lacks duration adverbials and denotes a punctual non-event rather than a non-terminating state or habitual series.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'had not been born' presents the transition event of birth as a bounded, completed whole (a transition from non-existence to existence), construed as a single event rather than an ongoing or habitual situation.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had not been born' appears in a counterfactual conditional construction ('It would have been better if that man had not been born'), which presents the situation as hypothetical and contrary to fact rather than asserted as realized.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'had not been born' contains the morphologically past auxiliary 'had' combined with 'been' followed by a past participle, instantiating both past perfect and progressive perfect features that distinguish it from simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had not been born' instantiates VcPass: it contains the auxiliary sequence 'had' + 'been' + past participle 'born' (of transitive verb 'bear'), the grammatical subject 'that man' is the semantic patient (the entity brought into existence), and overt passive morphology is present.

FullText: As he spoke , Judas , one of the twelve , followed by a crowd carrying swords and spears , approached him . The chief priest and elders of the people had sent them .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'had sent' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('The chief priest and elders of the people') and an overt direct object ('them'), satisfying the two-participant transitive pattern.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'send' licenses an external argument (the chief priest and elders as agent) and an internal argument (them as theme), making it a transitive verb that does not meet the unaccusative criteria.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'had sent' is transitive and licenses an internal argument ('them'), so it does not instantiate ASUnrg which requires no internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The event 'had sent them' is a punctual, instantaneous act of transmission that does not involve a durative process unfolding toward a defined endpoint; sending is a single discrete action without an inherent process phase or measurable duration leading to completion.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'had sent' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (the act of sending is completed at a definable boundary point), can be modified by specific time-point adverbials (e.g., 'had sent at dawn'), and does not permit interruption of an ongoing process phase in the same way durative verbs do.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'had sent' denotes a punctual, telic action with an inherent endpoint (the completion of sending) and a defined result state (them being sent), not a dynamic durative activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The past perfect 'had sent' entails a resulting change of state (the priests caused them to be sent/dispatched) and does not construe a single punctual dynamic occurrence without consequent result.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'had sent' denotes a bounded, completed dynamic event (sending) with an inherent endpoint and internal progression, not a state that holds over time without change.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'The chief priest and elders of the people had sent them' is a factual past statement describing an event that occurred in the narrative world, not a counterfactual condition or unfulfilled wish.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had sent' is a positive past perfect construction with no negation markers (not/n't), negative quantifiers, 'never', or 'neither...nor' constructions present.
DtExp_explanation: The clause 'had sent them' introduces a resulting state (the crowd being sent/dispatched) that is relevant for the narrative progression—explaining why the crowd approached Jesus—rather than focusing on the occurrence of sending as a life-history entry.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'had sent' describes a change of possession/delegation of authority where the chief priests and elders caused the crowd to be sent, and this resulting state (the crowd being sent and approaching) persists at the reference time of the narrative.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'had sent them' describes a discrete, completed action (the sending of the crowd) rather than a continuous state or activity extending to the reference time, lacking duration adverbials or indication of a non-terminating situation.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The past perfect 'had sent' presents the sending as a single bounded, completed event that occurred prior to the narrative sequence (the approach of Judas and the crowd), functioning as a discrete causal step in the narrative.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had sent' is a past perfect form asserting a realized fact in the actual world (the chief priests and elders had completed the action of sending), not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past, making this a past perfect form rather than a simple present perfect, which satisfies the first set_to_1_if condition.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had sent' is active voice (agent 'chief priest and elders' as subject, patient 'them' as object), lacking the required passive morphology of 'have' + 'been' + past participle.

FullText: » This has taken place so that the writings of the prophets will come true . « Then all his disciples left him .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb phrase 'has taken place' is an intransitive construction with no overt direct object; 'place' is part of the idiomatic verb phrase, not a grammatical object, and there is no internal argument affected by the event.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'take place' denotes an occurrence/happening event where the sole argument (the event itself, implicit) functions as an internal argument and no external argument is licensed, matching the criterion for existence/appearance/occurrence verbs.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb phrase 'has taken place' is an unaccusative construction where the subject 'this' functions as an internal argument (theme/patient) undergoing a change of state, not as an external argument (agent/initiator) of a one-participant event.
AktAcc_explanation: The event 'has taken place' denotes a punctual boundary transition marking the occurrence of an event at a single moment, without an inherent durative process phase or directed culmination toward a defined result state.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has taken place' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (an event occurring at a definable temporal boundary) and cannot be interrupted mid-occurrence, satisfying the criteria for achievement aspect.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has taken place' denotes a punctual, bounded event with an inherent endpoint and result state (something occurred and is now complete), not a dynamic durative situation that unfolds without an endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The phrase 'has taken place' entails a resulting change of state (an event has occurred and now exists as a completed fact), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status according to the CRI requirement that semelfactives must not entail a resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The verb phrase 'has taken place' denotes a bounded, completed event with internal dynamic development and an inherent endpoint, not a stative situation that holds uniformly over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'has taken place' describes a factual event that occurred in the actual world, not a counterfactual situation contrary to fact, and does not appear in a counterfactual conditional structure.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has taken place' contains no negation marker ('not'/'n't'), negative quantifier, 'never', or 'neither...nor' construction; the event is presented as realized rather than absent.
DtExp_explanation: The clause 'has taken place' introduces a result state (the fulfillment of prophecy) that is relevant for subsequent discourse ('so that the writings of the prophets will come true'), making it narrative progression rather than experiential.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'take place' describes an event that has occurred and the resulting state (that the event has happened) persists at the reference time, indicating a change of state with current relevance.
DtUniv_explanation: The predicate 'has taken place' denotes a punctual, completed event without duration adverbials or indication of a continuous state extending to the reference time, matching the negative example pattern of bounded/punctual results.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has taken place' is a transition event (occurrence/happening) construed as a single bounded completion, presenting the situation as a discrete event rather than ongoing or habitual.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has taken place' is a present perfect asserting a realized fact in the actual world, not a projected or hypothetical situation; the subordinate clause 'so that the writings of the prophets will come true' contains irrealis (future 'will'), but the target gram itself is realis.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has taken place' is a simple present perfect (auxiliary 'has' + past participle 'taken'), with no past morphology, future marking, modal auxiliary, or progressive 'been' structure.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'has taken place' is active voice (auxiliary 'have' + past participle of an intransitive verb), not passive voice; passive voice requires 'have' + 'been' + past participle of a transitive verb.

FullText: Then the high priest ripped his robes apart and said : » He has said evil against God ! Why do we need more witnesses ? You have heard his words of blasphemy ! «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have heard' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('You') and an overt direct object ('his words of blasphemy'), satisfying the first condition for ASTrns.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'hear' licenses an external argument (the subject 'you' as the experiencer/agent perceiving the words), making it not unaccusative; the sole argument does not function as an internal theme/patient undergoing a change of state or motion.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'heard' licenses an internal argument ('his words of blasphemy'), making it transitive and thus not an unergative one-participant event.
AktAcc_explanation: The event 'have heard' denotes a punctual perceptual transition into awareness (the moment of perceiving/becoming aware of the words), not a durative process culminating in a defined result state, and cannot be naturally modified by 'in X time' adverbials.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have heard' denotes a punctual perceptual transition into awareness of the blasphemous words, matching the positive example 'They have heard the news' and satisfying the criterion for punctual perceptual/cognitive transitions into recognition.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have heard' denotes a bounded perceptual event with a defined result state (perceiving and comprehending blasphemous words), not a dynamic durative activity; it matches the negative example 'They have heard the blasphemy.'
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have heard' entails a resulting change of state (transition from not knowing to knowing the words), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status; additionally, hearing is a durative perceptual process rather than a punctual instantaneous occurrence.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have heard' denotes a bounded perceptual event with a specific endpoint (the moment of hearing), not a stative situation that holds uniformly over time without dynamic change.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'have heard' is a factual statement in a direct quote describing a past event that actually occurred (the witnesses heard the blasphemous words), not a counterfactual condition or unfulfilled wish.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have heard' is not negated by 'not'/'n't', does not contain a negative quantifier, is not modified by 'never', and does not appear in a 'neither…nor' construction; it presents the event as realized rather than absent.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have heard' denotes perception where the focus is on the occurrence of the experience (hearing the blasphemous words) at least once in the subject's life history, rather than on a resulting state.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have heard' reports a past perceptual event without entailing a persisting resultant state; the act of hearing itself does not create a change of state that holds at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'You have heard his words of blasphemy' describes a completed perceptual event (hearing) with a specific object, not a continuous state or habitual series extending to the reference time, thus failing to meet DtUniv criteria.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'have heard' denotes a stative/experiential situation (perception of words) rather than a bounded transition; it presents an unbounded state of having access to information, not a single completed event with a construed endpoint.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have heard' is a present perfect asserting a realized fact in the actual world—the witnesses have actually heard the blasphemous words—not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'have heard' is a simple present perfect (auxiliary 'have' + past participle 'heard') with no past morphology, future marking, modal auxiliary, or progressive aspect, thus it does not instantiate TLTS.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'have heard' is active voice (you as agent, his words as patient), lacking the required passive morphology of 'have been' + past participle.

FullText: He said : » I have done wrong in giving a righteous man to you . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb phrase 'have done wrong' lacks an overt direct object; 'wrong' functions as a predicative complement describing the action, not as a patient argument, so the construction does not instantiate the two-participant transitive pattern required for ASTrns.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'do wrong' licenses an external argument (the speaker 'I') that functions as an agent performing a volitional action, not as an internal argument undergoing a change of state or motion.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb phrase 'have done wrong' licenses an internal argument ('wrong' as a cognate object or complement), and the infinitival phrase 'in giving a righteous man to you' further specifies the content of the wrongdoing, indicating this is not a simple one-participant unergative construction.
AktAcc_explanation: The event 'have done wrong' denotes a punctual, instantaneous transgression or moral failure without an inherent durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint; it describes a completed action rather than a gradual change-of-state or directed process toward a goal.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have done wrong' denotes a durative action or state of wrongdoing rather than a punctual transition into a new state; it describes an extended morally culpable action, not a boundary-crossing moment, and cannot be naturally modified by a specific time-point adverbial in the same way achievements can.
AktAct_explanation: The phrase 'have done wrong' denotes a completed action with a defined result state (having committed a wrongdoing), not a dynamic durative situation without inherent endpoint, and thus does not instantiate AktAct.
AktSemel_explanation: The phrase 'have done wrong' entails a resulting change of state (the wrongdoing has occurred and persists as a completed action with consequences), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status; it is a perfective accomplishment, not a punctual event without change of state.
AktState_explanation: The verb phrase 'have done wrong' denotes a completed dynamic action with internal progression and a bounded endpoint, not a stative situation that holds uniformly over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'have done wrong' occurs in a reported speech context expressing a factual acknowledgment of a past action, not in a counterfactual conditional protasis, apodosis, unfulfilled wish, or inverted conditional structure.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have done wrong' contains no negation marker ('not'/'n't'), negative quantifier, 'never', or 'neither...nor' construction; the event is presented as realized, not absent from reality.
DtExp_explanation: The negative Perfect 'have done wrong' functions as a claim about a wrongful action in the subject's history, focusing on the occurrence of the event rather than a resulting state, which aligns with the experiential interpretation.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have done wrong' describes a change of state (from not having wronged to having wronged) with the resulting state of culpability/wrongdoing currently in effect at the reference time, meeting the criterion for resultative aspect.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'have done wrong' describes a punctual, completed action (wrongdoing) rather than a continuous state or habitual series extending to the reference time, lacking duration adverbials or iterative markers required for DtUniv classification.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'I have done wrong' presents a completed action as a bounded whole with a construed endpoint, fitting the perfective aspect profile of a transition/completion event asserted as a finished occurrence.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have done wrong' is a present perfect form asserting a realized fact within the speaker's reported speech; it lacks modal auxiliaries, conditional/counterfactual marking, or subjunctive/optative context that would project it as non-actual.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'have done wrong' is a simple present perfect (have + past participle) with no past auxiliary, future marking, modal auxiliary, or progressive aspect, so it does not instantiate TLTS.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'have done wrong' lacks the required passive morphology (auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle); it is active voice with 'have' as a perfect auxiliary and 'done' as a past participle of a transitive verb, but without 'been' interposed, and the subject 'I' is the semantic agent, not the patient.

FullText: While he was seated in judgment his wife sent a message to him . She said , » Do not judge that righteous man . I have had much trouble this day in a dream because of him . «
ASTrns_explanation: The target verb 'have had' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('I') and an overt direct object ('much trouble'), satisfying the first condition for ASTrns.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'have' (in 'have had') licenses an external argument (the experiencer 'I') and an internal argument ('much trouble'), making it a transitive verb that does not meet the unaccusative criteria.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'have had' licenses an internal argument ('much trouble'), making it transitive rather than a one-participant unergative event.
AktAcc_explanation: The event 'have had much trouble' denotes a durative experiential state that unfolded over the course of the day ('this day') and culminated in a defined result condition (the trouble experienced), fitting the pattern of a gradual accumulation of distress leading to a bounded endpoint.
AktAch_explanation: The verb phrase 'have had much trouble' denotes a durative state of experiencing difficulty over an extended period ('this day'), not a punctual transition into a new state, and does not permit modification by a specific time-point adverbial in the same way achievements do.
AktAct_explanation: The phrase 'have had much trouble' denotes a bounded, resultant state (trouble experienced and completed during a specific time period) rather than a dynamic, durative activity without inherent endpoint; the internal argument 'much trouble' is quantized and bounded, entailing a defined result state.
AktSemel_explanation: The phrase 'have had much trouble' describes a state or condition resulting from a dream experience, not a single punctual dynamic occurrence; it entails a resulting state of distress and does not meet the semelfactive criteria of a bounded instantaneous event without change of state.
AktState_explanation: The present perfect 'have had much trouble' denotes a bounded, completed experiential event (trouble occurring during a dream) rather than a stative situation holding over time without dynamic change.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'I have had much trouble this day in a dream because of him' describes a factual past event that actually occurred (the wife experienced a troubling dream), not a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have had much trouble' contains no negation markers (not, n't, never) and no negative quantifiers; it is a positive assertion of a realized state.
DtExp_explanation: The negative experiential perfect 'have had much trouble' presents a bounded experience (trouble in a dream) as part of the subject's life history without asserting a persisting result state, focusing on the occurrence of the experience itself.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have had trouble' describes a durative experiential state (suffering trouble during a dream) rather than a change of state with a persisting result; the trouble occurred in the past (in a dream) and does not entail a resulting state currently in effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'I have had much trouble this day in a dream' describes a bounded, punctual experience occurring within a single day rather than a continuous state or activity extending to the reference time, lacking duration adverbials that would indicate extension throughout a time span.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'I have had much trouble this day in a dream because of him' presents trouble as an unbounded state or experience rather than a bounded transition; 'have had' with a stative predicate (trouble) does not construe the situation as a single completed event with a clear endpoint.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have had much trouble' is a present perfect form asserting a realized state of affairs in the actual world (the wife's experienced trouble during the dream), not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'have had' instantiates the past perfect form (auxiliary 'have' + past participle 'had'), which encodes a tense specification other than simple present perfect, matching the first set_to_1_if criterion.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'have had much trouble' is active voice (present perfect of the transitive verb 'have'), not passive voice; the subject 'I' is the agent/experiencer, not the semantic patient, and there is no 'been' + past participle passive morphology.

FullText: » What evil has he done ? « Pilate asked . But they shouted : » Impale him ! «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'done' has an overt grammatical subject 'he' and an overt direct object 'evil', satisfying the first condition for ASTrns marking.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'do' with object 'evil' licenses an external argument (the subject 'he'), making this a transitive construction that does not meet the unaccusative criteria.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'done' licenses an internal argument ('evil'), making this a transitive construction that does not instantiate ASUnrg, which requires no internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The event 'do evil' involves a durative process of committing harmful acts that culminates in a defined result state (evil having been done), and can be naturally modified by 'in X time' adverbials (e.g., 'What evil has he done in three years?'), satisfying the criteria for accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'done' denotes a punctual transition into a completed state of wrongdoing; the event can be modified by specific time-point adverbials (e.g., 'What evil has he done today?') and does not permit interruption of an ongoing process phase, satisfying the criteria for achievement aspect.
AktAct_explanation: The verb phrase 'have done evil' denotes a completed action with a defined result state (evil that has been committed), not a dynamic durative situation without inherent endpoint, and thus does not instantiate AktAct.
AktSemel_explanation: The phrase 'have done evil' entails a resulting change of state (harm caused) and does not represent a single punctual occurrence without consequent result, thus it is not semelfactive.
AktState_explanation: The verb phrase 'have done evil' denotes a bounded, completed dynamic event (a past accomplishment) rather than a state that holds over time without change or progression.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The phrase 'have done evil' occurs in a factual interrogative clause ('What evil has he done?') asking about actual events that occurred, not in a counterfactual conditional, apodosis, unfulfilled wish, or inverted conditional structure.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has done evil' is not negated; it appears in a question asking what evil he has done, which presupposes the occurrence of evil actions rather than negating them.
DtExp_explanation: The negative perfect 'has he done evil' functions as a claim about the absence or presence of an event in the subject's life history, answering whether the event has occurred at least once, which instantiates the DtExp feature.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'do' with the object 'evil' reports past actions without entailing a persisting resultant state; it is a past interaction query rather than a change of state that holds at reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'What evil has he done?' describes a bounded, punctual accomplishment (specific evil acts completed) rather than a continuous state or habitual series extending to the reference time, lacking duration adverbials or non-terminating situation markers.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'has he done evil' presents a completed bounded event (the doing of evil as a whole) rather than an ongoing or habitual state, matching the perfective construal of a transition/accomplishment predicate.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have done evil' appears in an interrogative clause ('What evil has he done?') that seeks confirmation of an actual fact in the narrative world, not a projected or hypothetical situation, thus instantiating realis rather than irrealis.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'have done evil' is a simple present perfect (have + past participle 'done') with no past auxiliary, future marking, modal auxiliary, or progressive 'been' construction, so it does not instantiate TLTS.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'has done' is active voice (auxiliary 'have' + past participle 'done'), not passive voice; the grammatical subject 'he' is the agent performing the action, not the semantic patient.

FullText: About the ninth hour Jesus cried with a loud voice , » Eli , Eli , lama sabachthani ? « Which translated means : » My God , my God , why have you forsaken me ? «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have forsaken' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('you') and an overt direct object ('me'), instantiating the two-participant transitive pattern required for ASTrns.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'forsaken' (past participle of 'forsake') licenses an external argument 'you' (the agent who abandons) and an internal argument 'me' (the patient/theme), making it a transitive verb that does not meet the unaccusative criteria.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'forsaken' is transitive and licenses an internal argument ('me'), so it does not instantiate ASUnrg which requires no internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'forsaken' denotes a punctual state-change (transition from not-abandoned to abandoned) rather than a durative process with an inherent culmination phase; it marks a boundary transition into a result state without measurable duration or process unfolding.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have forsaken' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (abandonment/separation) that can be modified by specific time-point adverbials and does not permit interruption of an ongoing process phase, meeting the criteria for achievement aspect.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have forsaken' denotes a completed state change with a defined result (abandonment/separation), not a dynamic durative activity that unfolds without an inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have forsaken' entails a resulting change of state (abandonment/separation) and is not a punctual dynamic occurrence without consequent result, thus it does not instantiate AktSemel.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have forsaken' denotes a dynamic change of state (abandonment/rejection) with an inherent endpoint and internal progression, not a static situation that holds uniformly over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'why have you forsaken me' is a factual utterance expressing a real situation Jesus is experiencing in the moment, not a counterfactual condition or unfulfilled wish contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have forsaken' is not negated; it appears in a positive interrogative construction ('why have you forsaken me?') expressing a realized state rather than absence.
DtExp_explanation: The negative Perfect 'have you forsaken me' functions as a claim about the absence of the expected supportive action in the subject's (God's) history, fitting the criterion for negative Perfects that instantiate DtExp.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'forsaken' describes a change of state (abandonment/withdrawal of support), and the clause indicates this resulting state of being abandoned currently holds at the reference time of Jesus's utterance.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'why have you forsaken me' describes a completed action or state change (abandonment) rather than a continuous situation extending to the reference time, lacking duration adverbials or habitual iteration that would indicate DtUniv.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'why have you forsaken me' presents forsaking as a completed transition event (abandonment as a bounded whole) rather than an ongoing state, matching the perfective construal of a transition predicate asserted as a completed occurrence.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The phrase 'have you forsaken me' is a direct utterance asserting a realized state of affairs in the actual world (Jesus's experience of abandonment), not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'have forsaken' is a simple present perfect form (auxiliary 'have' + past participle 'forsaken') with no past, future, modal, or progressive marking, thus does not instantiate TLTS.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'have forsaken' is active voice (agent 'you' as subject performing the action), not passive voice; passive voice requires the auxiliary sequence 'have' + 'been' + past participle.

FullText: He laid it in his own new tomb , which he had cut out of the rock . Then he rolled a great stone in front of the door of the tomb , and departed .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'cut' has an overt grammatical subject 'he' and an overt direct object 'it' (referring to the tomb), satisfying the requirement for a two-participant transitive construction with both external and internal arguments.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'cut' licenses an external argument (the agent 'he' who performs the cutting action), making it transitive and not unaccusative.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'cut' licenses an internal argument ('out of the rock'), making it transitive rather than a one-participant unergative event.
AktAcc_explanation: The event 'cut out of the rock' involves a durative process of carving/excavating directed toward the defined goal of creating a tomb, naturally modifiable by 'in X time' (e.g., 'cut out in weeks'), instantiating AktAcc.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'cut out' denotes an extended process of carving stone rather than a punctual transition; it lacks the instantaneous boundary quality required for AktAch and permits interruption of the ongoing carving phase.
AktAct_explanation: The verb phrase 'had cut out of' denotes a completed action with an inherent endpoint (a finished tomb carved from rock), not a dynamic durative situation without a defined goal.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had cut out of the rock' entails a resulting change of state (the rock is now carved into a tomb shape), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status; additionally, the action is construed as a completed process rather than a single punctual occurrence.
AktState_explanation: The verb phrase 'had cut out of' denotes a completed dynamic action (carving stone) with internal progression and a defined endpoint, not a stative situation holding over time without change.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'which he had cut out of the rock' is a factual past perfect describing a completed action in narrative sequence, not a counterfactual condition or unfulfilled wish.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had cut out of' contains no negation markers (not, n't, never) and no negative quantifiers; the event is presented as realized rather than absent.
DtExp_explanation: The past perfect 'had cut out of' describes a completed action whose resulting state (a tomb existing in the rock) is directly relevant to the subsequent narrative action of rolling a stone in front of it, making it non-experiential.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'cut out' describes a change of state (rock transformed from uncut to cut/carved), and the resulting state (a tomb carved from rock) persists at the reference time as the location where the body is laid.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'which he had cut out of the rock' describes a completed punctual action (carving a tomb) prior to the reference time, not a continuous state or habitual series extending to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'had cut out of' is a transition event (cutting/carving) construed as a completed bounded whole in past time, presented as a discrete prior action that enabled the subsequent narrative events.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had cut out of' is a past perfect form asserting a realized action in the actual world (he actually cut the tomb), not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past, making this a past perfect form rather than a simple present perfect, which satisfies the first set_to_1_if condition.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had cut out of' is active voice (subject 'he' is the agent performing the action), not passive voice; it lacks the required 'been' + past participle structure for passive morphology.

FullText: The angel spoke to the women : » I know you seek Jesus who has been impaled ; do not fear .
ASTrns_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('has been impaled'), and the lexical verb 'impale' licenses both an external argument (agent—the one who impaled) and an internal argument (patient—Jesus, the one impaled), satisfying the passive condition in set_to_1_if.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'impale' in passive voice 'has been impaled' licenses an external argument (an implicit agent performing the impalement), which disqualifies it from ASUncc status according to the rule that unaccusative verbs do not license external arguments.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'impaled' is in passive voice with an internal argument (Jesus as the logical object), so it does not instantiate ASUnrg which requires no internal argument licensed.
AktAcc_explanation: The event 'impale' is a punctual, instantaneous action that reaches its terminal state at a single boundary moment (the piercing through), not a durative process unfolding toward completion, and cannot be naturally modified by 'in X time' adverbials measuring the event's duration.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'impale' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (being pierced/affixed), represents a definable threshold crossing that cannot be interrupted mid-process, and naturally permits time-point modification (e.g., 'was impaled at dawn'), satisfying the achievement criteria.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'impale' denotes a punctual, telic event with an inherent endpoint and defined result state (being pierced through), not a dynamic durative activity that unfolds without completion.
AktSemel_explanation: The verb 'has been impaled' entails a resulting change of state (the person is now in an impaled condition), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status according to the CRIs.
AktState_explanation: The verb phrase 'has been impaled' denotes a bounded, completed dynamic event (the action of impaling) resulting in a state, not a stative situation that holds without internal change or progression.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'who has been impaled' is a factual relative clause describing an actual event that occurred (Jesus's crucifixion), not a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has been impaled' contains no negation markers (not/n't/never) and is not modified by negative quantifiers or 'neither...nor' constructions; the negation in the sentence ('do not fear') applies to a different clause.
DtExp_explanation: The phrase 'has been impaled' introduces a resulting state (Jesus being in a state of having been impaled/dead) that is relevant for the subsequent discourse, functioning as narrative progression to set the scene rather than as an experiential entry in a life history.
DtRes_explanation: The construction 'has been impaled' is a stative copula (present perfect of 'be' + past participle used adjectivally) that describes Jesus's current state rather than encoding a transition from one state to another, per the edge case rule for stative copulas.
DtUniv_explanation: The predicate 'has been impaled' denotes a punctual, completed action resulting in a state change (transition from alive to impaled), not a continuous situation extending to the reference time, and lacks duration adverbials or habitual iteration markers required for DtUniv.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The passive form 'has been impaled' construes a bounded transition event (the act of impalement) as a completed whole, matching the criterion for passive bounded transitions.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has been impaled' is a present perfect indicative asserting a realized fact in the actual world (Jesus's state of being impaled), not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has been impaled' includes 'been' followed by a progressive participle (past participle used in passive voice construction), which matches the set_to_1_if criterion for progressive perfect forms.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has been impaled' instantiates VcPass: it contains the auxiliary sequence 'have' + 'been' + past participle of the transitive verb 'impale', the grammatical subject 'Jesus' is the semantic patient (the entity affected by the impaling), and the construction exhibits overt passive morphology.

FullText: » He is not here , for he has risen just as he promised . Come ; see the place where the Lord lay .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has risen' is intransitive with no overt direct object or internal argument; it describes a one-participant event (the subject 'he') without an affected patient, thus failing all conditions for ASTrns marking.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'risen' denotes directed motion/change-of-location (upward displacement) where the sole argument 'he' functions as an internal argument (theme) undergoing displacement, with no external argument licensed, matching the unaccusative pattern.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'risen' is unaccusative (change-of-state), where the subject 'he' functions as an internal argument undergoing the state change rather than as an external agent/initiator, thus failing the ASUnrg criteria.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'has risen' denotes a punctual transition from a state of being dead/laid to a state of being risen, without an inherent durative process phase or measurable duration leading to completion.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has risen' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (from dead to alive/resurrected), is naturally modifiable by time-point adverbials, cannot be interrupted mid-occurrence, and lacks measurable internal duration, satisfying multiple set_to_1_if criteria.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has risen' denotes a change of state with an inherent endpoint (transition from lying to risen position) and a defined result state, not a dynamic durative activity that unfolds without completion.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has risen' entails a resulting change of state (from dead to alive/risen), which disqualifies it from being a semelfactive; semelfactives must be dynamic punctual events without consequent result conditions.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has risen' denotes a dynamic, bounded event of change (transition from lying to risen state) with an inherent endpoint, not a stative situation that holds uniformly over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'he has risen just as he promised' describes a factual event that has occurred in the actual world (not contrary to fact), supported by the context 'Come; see the place where the Lord lay,' which confirms the event as real and verifiable.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has risen' is not negated; the negation 'not' applies to the matrix clause 'He is not here' rather than to the target gram, which presents the rising as a realized positive event.
DtExp_explanation: The perfect 'has risen' introduces a resulting state (Jesus is no longer in the tomb) that is relevant for the subsequent discourse ('Come; see the place where the Lord lay'), making it non-experiential rather than a life-history entry.
DtRes_explanation: The motion verb 'risen' describes a change of location (from lying dead to risen/ascended), and the context 'He is not here, for he has risen' indicates the resulting state (risen/ascended condition) persists at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The predicate 'has risen' denotes a punctual, completed action (resurrection) rather than a continuous state or activity extending to the reference time, lacking duration adverbials or habitual iteration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has risen' is a transition event (change of state from dead to alive) construed as a completed whole, and the clause presents this as a single bounded occurrence with a construed endpoint, matching the perfective aspect criteria.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has risen' is asserted as a realized fact in the actual world (he has actually risen), not presented as projected, hypothetical, or non-actual; the negation of 'is not here' and the causal 'for' clause confirm this as a statement of realized fact.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'has risen' is a simple present perfect (auxiliary 'has' + past participle 'risen') with no past morphology, future marking, modal auxiliary, or progressive aspect, so it does not instantiate TLTS.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has risen' is active voice (auxiliary 'have' + past participle of intransitive verb 'rise'), not passive voice; it lacks the required 'been' element and the subject 'he' is the semantic agent/experiencer, not the patient.

FullText: » Go quickly and tell his disciples he has risen from the dead . See , he goes before you to Galilee . You will see him there , as I have told you . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has risen' is intransitive with no overt direct object; it takes only an external argument (subject 'he') and does not license an internal argument, failing all three set_to_1 conditions.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'risen' denotes a change-of-location/change-of-state event (rising from the dead) where the sole argument 'he' functions as an internal argument (theme/patient) undergoing displacement, with no external argument licensed.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'risen' is unaccusative (change-of-state), where the subject 'he' functions as an internal argument undergoing the state change rather than as an external agent/initiator, which disqualifies it from ASUnrg.
AktAcc_explanation: The event 'has risen from the dead' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (from dead to alive) rather than a durative process with a measurable duration leading to completion; it marks a boundary crossing, not an accomplishment with an inherent process phase.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has risen' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (from dead to alive), lacks measurable internal duration, and represents a definable threshold crossing that cannot be interrupted or partially completed.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has risen' denotes a change of state with an inherent endpoint (transition from dead to alive) and a defined result state, not a dynamic durative activity that unfolds without completion.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has risen from the dead' entails a resulting change of state (transition from dead to alive), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status despite its punctual construal.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has risen' denotes a bounded dynamic event (resurrection) with internal progression and an inherent endpoint, not a stative situation that holds uniformly over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'he has risen from the dead' describes a factual event presented as having occurred in reality, not a counterfactual situation contrary to fact; it is a statement of fact within a narrative, not a conditional protasis, apodosis, unfulfilled wish, or explicitly marked contrary-to-fact situation.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has risen' is not negated by 'not'/'n't', does not contain a negative quantifier, is not modified by 'never', and does not appear in a 'neither…nor' construction; it presents the event as occurring in reality.
DtExp_explanation: The clause 'has risen from the dead' introduces a resulting state (Jesus is now alive/present) that is relevant for subsequent discourse ('he goes before you'), making it non-experiential rather than a life-history entry.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has risen' describes a change of state (from dead to alive) where the resulting state (being alive/risen) persists at the reference time, as confirmed by the subsequent clause 'he goes before you to Galilee' indicating current presence and activity.
DtUniv_explanation: The predicate 'has risen' denotes a punctual, completed action (resurrection) resulting in a new state, not a continuous activity or habitual series extending to the reference time, and lacks duration adverbials indicating extension.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has risen' is a transition event (change of state from dead to alive) construed as a completed whole, presenting the situation as a single bounded occurrence with a construed endpoint.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has risen' is asserted as a realized fact in the actual world within the reported speech, not presented as projected, hypothetical, or uncertain.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'has risen' is a simple present perfect (auxiliary 'has' + past participle 'risen') with no past, future, modal, or progressive marking, thus does not instantiate TLTS.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has risen' is active voice (auxiliary 'have' + present participle of intransitive verb 'rise'), not passive voice; passive voice requires 'have' + 'been' + past participle of a transitive verb.

FullText: » Go quickly and tell his disciples he has risen from the dead . See , he goes before you to Galilee . You will see him there , as I have told you . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have told' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('I') and an overt direct object ('you'), satisfying the first condition for ASTrns.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'tell' licenses an external argument (the speaker 'I') and an internal argument (the addressee 'you'), making it a transitive verb that does not meet the unaccusative criteria of a sole internal argument with no external argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'told' is transitive and licenses an internal argument (the content 'you'), making it a two-participant event rather than a one-participant unergative event.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'have told' denotes a punctual speech act of communication that is completed at a single moment of utterance, lacking a durative process phase directed toward a defined endpoint.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have told' denotes a punctual perceptual/cognitive transition into awareness (the disciples becoming aware of information), can be modified by specific time-point adverbials ('as I have told you at that moment'), and represents a boundary transition of communication rather than an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have told' denotes a completed communicative act with a defined result state (information conveyed to the addressee), not a dynamic durative situation that unfolds without an inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The present perfect 'have told' entails a resulting change of state (the disciples now possess the information that was communicated) and does not construe the event as a single punctual occurrence without consequence.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have told' denotes a completed communicative act (past event) rather than a state that holds over time; it involves dynamic volitional activity and has an inherent endpoint, failing all criteria for AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'as I have told you' is a factual past statement describing something that actually occurred (a prior telling), not a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have told' contains no negation marker ('not', 'n't'), negative quantifier, 'never', or 'neither...nor' construction; it presents the event as realized rather than absent.
DtExp_explanation: The perfect 'have told' introduces a resulting state of knowledge in the addressees (they now know what was told), which is relevant for the subsequent discourse about seeing Jesus in Galilee, rather than focusing on the occurrence of the telling event in the subject's life history.
DtRes_explanation: The communication verb 'have told' merely reports a past interaction (prior instruction) without entailing that the information is currently binding or in effect; the clause does not indicate a persisting resultative state.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'as I have told you' describes a completed past action of telling rather than a continuous state or habitual series extending to the reference time, lacking duration adverbials or non-terminating situation indicators required for DtUniv.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'as I have told you' presents a stative/unbounded situation of prior communication rather than a bounded transition event; it asserts a completed action but does not construe it as a discrete bounded occurrence with a profiled endpoint in the narrative sequence.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have told' is a present perfect asserting a realized fact in the actual world (a past communication that has occurred), not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'have told' is a simple present perfect (auxiliary 'have' + past participle 'told') with no past morphology, future marking, modal auxiliary, or progressive aspect, thus it does not instantiate TLTS.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have told' is active voice (subject 'I' is the agent performing the telling), not passive voice; passive voice requires the auxiliary sequence 'have' + 'been' + past participle.

FullText: Jesus came to them and spoke to them , saying : » All authority has been given to me in heaven and on earth .
ASTrns_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('has been given'), and the lexical verb 'give' licenses both an external argument (agent, unexpressed) and an internal argument (patient: 'All authority'), satisfying the passive condition in set_to_1_if.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'has been given' is a passive construction that licenses an external argument (the implicit agent 'by someone/God'), and the sole overt argument 'authority' functions as the theme/patient of an external causer, not as an unaccusative internal argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The target 'has been given' is a passive construction that licenses an internal argument ('All authority'), making it transitive rather than a one-participant unergative event.
AktAcc_explanation: The event 'has been given' denotes a punctual transfer of authority that culminates instantaneously in a result state, without an inherent durative process phase leading to completion.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has been given' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (authority transferred to Jesus) that can be modified by a specific time-point adverbial and does not permit interruption of an ongoing process phase, satisfying the criteria for achievement aspect.
AktAct_explanation: The passive construction 'has been given' denotes a bounded transfer event with an inherent result state (authority transferred to Jesus), not a dynamic durative activity without an endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has been given' entails a resulting change of state (authority transferred to Jesus) and is not a punctual dynamic occurrence without consequent result, thus it does not instantiate AktSemel.
AktState_explanation: The passive perfect 'has been given' denotes a bounded dynamic event (the act of giving authority) with a resultant state, not a stative situation that holds uniformly over time without internal change.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'All authority has been given to me' is a factual statement of a completed action in the actual world, not a counterfactual condition or unfulfilled wish, and contains no markers of contrary-to-fact meaning.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has been given' is not negated by 'not'/'n't', does not contain a negative quantifier in subject or object position, is not modified by 'never', and does not appear in a 'neither…nor' construction; the event is presented as realized, not absent.
DtExp_explanation: The passive perfect 'has been given' introduces a resulting state (Jesus possessing authority) that is relevant to the subsequent discourse, not a life-history experiential event.
DtRes_explanation: The construction 'has been given' is a stative copula (passive perfect copula) describing a state of possession rather than encoding a transition; per edge case rules, copular constructions do not encode the change itself but only the resulting state.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'All authority has been given to me' describes a completed transfer resulting in a new state, not a continuous activity or habitual series extending to the reference time, and lacks duration adverbials indicating extension.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The passive form 'has been given' presents a bounded transition event (the conferral of authority) as a completed whole, matching the criterion for passive bounded transitions.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has been given' is a present perfect passive construction asserting a realized state of affairs in the actual world (authority has been given to Jesus), not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The gram 'has been given' includes 'been' followed by a progressive participle form (past participle 'given' functioning in a progressive-like construction), matching the set_to_1_if criterion for progressive perfect forms.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has been given' instantiates the passive voice with the auxiliary sequence 'have' + 'been' + past participle of the transitive verb 'give', the grammatical subject 'All authority' corresponds to the semantic patient, and the agent is omitted.

FullText: » Teach them to observe all the things I have commanded you . Behold , I am with you always , even to the end of the age . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have commanded' introduces an overt external argument (I) and an overt internal argument (you as direct object), satisfying the first set_to_1_if condition for a transitive construction with both subject and direct object.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'commanded' licenses an external argument (the speaker 'I' as agent) and an internal argument (the patient 'you'), making it a transitive verb that does not meet the unaccusative criteria.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'have commanded' licenses an internal argument ('you'), making it transitive and thus not an unergative one-participant event.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'have commanded' denotes a punctual speech act that establishes a directive at a single moment of utterance, lacking an inherent durative process phase or measurable duration leading to a defined result state.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have commanded' denotes a durative process of issuing multiple commands over time rather than a punctual transition into a new state, and does not naturally modify with a specific time-point adverbial indicating a boundary moment.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have commanded' denotes a completed action with a defined result state (a set of commands given), not a dynamic durative situation; it cannot be naturally modified by 'for X time' without implying incompleteness, and the internal argument 'all the things' is quantized/bounded, entailing completion.
AktSemel_explanation: The perfect aspect 'have commanded' entails a resulting change of state (the commands now exist as given instructions with ongoing relevance) and does not construe the event as a single punctual occurrence without consequent result.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have commanded' denotes a bounded, completed dynamic event (past action of issuing commands) rather than a state that holds over time without change or progression.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The phrase 'have commanded' describes a factual past action that has actually occurred, not a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have commanded' is not negated by 'not'/'n't', does not contain a negative quantifier, is not modified by 'never', and does not appear in a 'neither…nor' construction; it presents the event as realized rather than absent.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'have commanded' introduces a resulting state (a set of commandments that exist and must be observed), not merely the occurrence of the commanding event in the subject's life history.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have commanded' describes a change of status/directive (commands issued) that are currently binding and in effect, as evidenced by the imperative 'Teach them to observe all the things I have commanded you,' which presupposes the commands remain valid and enforceable at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The phrase 'have commanded' denotes a completed action with a resultant state (the commands now exist as given instructions), not a continuous activity or state extending to the reference time, and lacks duration adverbials or habitual iteration markers required for DtUniv classification.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'have commanded' presents a completed transition event (the act of commanding) as a bounded whole with a construed endpoint, fitting the perfective aspect profile.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have commanded' is a present perfect form asserting a realized action in the actual world (the speaker has already issued commands), not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'have commanded' is a simple present perfect (auxiliary 'have' + past participle 'commanded') with no past, future, modal, or progressive marking, thus does not instantiate TLTS.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have commanded' is active voice (subject 'I' is the agent performing the action), not passive voice; passive voice requires the auxiliary sequence 'have' + 'been' + past participle, which is absent here.

FullText: Jesus said : » The time has come . The kingdom of God is at hand . Repent and believe the good news . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has come' is intransitive with no overt direct object or internal argument, and does not meet any of the set_to_1_if conditions.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'come' denotes directed motion or change-of-location (arrival of time) where the sole argument 'The time' functions as an internal argument undergoing displacement, with no external argument licensed.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'come' is unaccusative with the subject 'The time' functioning as an internal argument (theme/patient) undergoing a change of state, not as an external argument (agent/initiator) of a volitional activity.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'has come' denotes a punctual boundary transition into a state (arrival at a defined moment) rather than a durative process with an inherent culmination phase, making it an achievement rather than an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has come' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (the arrival/occurrence of 'the time'), can be modified by specific time-point adverbials ('The time has come at that moment'), and represents a boundary crossing rather than an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has come' denotes a punctual event with an inherent endpoint and defined result state (arrival at a specific moment), not a dynamic durative situation, and cannot be naturally modified by 'for X time' adverbials without semantic oddity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has come' entails a resulting change of state (the time transitioning from not-arrived to arrived) and is not construed as a single punctual occurrence without consequent result, thus it is not semelfactive.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has come' denotes a bounded dynamic event (arrival/change of state) with an inherent endpoint, not a stative situation that holds uniformly over time without internal progression.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'The time has come' describes a situation presented as factually occurring in the actual world, not as contrary to fact; it lacks the conditional, modal perfect, or regret structures required for ClCntfcl annotation.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has come' is not negated by 'not'/'n't', does not contain a negative quantifier, is not modified by 'never', and does not appear in a 'neither…nor' construction; it presents the event as occurring in reality.
DtExp_explanation: The clause 'The time has come' introduces a current state of affairs (the time is now present) rather than presenting an event as part of a subject's life history, matching the negative example 'The time has come.'
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has come' describes a motion/arrival event where the time has moved to the present moment and that state (the time being now) persists at the reference time, instantiating a resultative meaning.
DtUniv_explanation: The predicate 'has come' denotes a punctual, perfective event (arrival/occurrence at a specific moment) rather than a continuous state or activity extending to the reference time, lacking duration adverbials or habitual iteration.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has come' is a transition event (arrival/occurrence) construed as a completed bounded whole, presenting the situation as a single occurrence with a construed endpoint rather than as ongoing.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has come' is a present perfect form asserting a realized fact in the actual world (the time has arrived), not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'has come' is a simple present perfect (auxiliary 'has' + past participle 'come') with no past, future, modal, or progressive marking, thus does not instantiate TLTS.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has come' is active voice (auxiliary 'have' + past participle of intransitive verb 'come'), not passive voice; passive voice requires 'have' + 'been' + past participle of a transitive verb.

FullText: » What do you want with us Jesus of Nazareth ? Have you come to destroy us ? I know who you are . You are the Holy One of God ! «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have come' is intransitive with no overt direct object; the infinitive phrase 'to destroy us' expresses purpose rather than making 'come' transitive, and 'us' is the object of 'destroy', not of 'come'.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'come' licenses an external argument (the subject 'you'), which functions as an agent with intentional directed motion, not as a theme/patient undergoing change-of-state or displacement without agency.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'come' is unaccusative (the subject 'you' is an internal argument undergoing a change of state/location), not an unergative verb with an external agent argument, and thus does not instantiate ASUnrg.
AktAcc_explanation: The event 'have come to destroy' is a punctual boundary transition marking arrival at a location, not a durative process with a measurable duration leading to completion; the infinitive 'to destroy' expresses purpose/intention rather than constituting the temporal structure of the main event itself.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have come' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (arrival at a location/moment), naturally modified by temporal boundaries, and represents a crossing of a definable threshold rather than an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: The target 'have come (to destroy)' denotes a punctual arrival event with an inherent endpoint and defined result state (reaching a location/moment), not a dynamic durative situation without inherent completion.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'have come (to destroy)' entails a resulting change of state (destruction) and is not construed as a single punctual occurrence without consequent result, thus it does not instantiate AktSemel.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have come' denotes a dynamic, bounded event of arrival with inherent progression and endpoint, not a stative situation holding over time without change.

FullText: He got up , took the bed and went out before them all . They were all filled with wonder . They gave glory to God and said we have never seen anything like this .

ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'seen' licenses an external argument (the implicit subject 'we'), making it a transitive verb with an agent, not an unaccusative verb.

DtRes_explanation: The verb 'seen' describes a perceptual experience without a persisting change of state; the clause reports a past interaction (never having witnessed such an event) without entailing that any resulting state is currently in effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'we have never seen anything like this' expresses a negative existential state (non-occurrence) rather than a continuous activity or habitual series extending to the reference time, lacking duration adverbials and denoting a bounded negation rather than a universal temporal span.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'we have never seen anything like this' presents a bounded negative existential state across a completed time span (from past to present), asserting that a particular situation has not occurred as a single bounded whole rather than describing an ongoing process.

FullText: Jesus heard this . He said to them : » Those who are well have no need of a medical man but those who are ill do . I have come to care for sinners not the upright . «

DtRes_explanation: The motion verb 'come' describes a change of location (arrival), and the context indicates Jesus has arrived at his current location/purpose and remains there at the reference time, satisfying the resultative condition for motion verbs.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'I have come to care for sinners' describes a punctual arrival event with a resulting purpose, not a continuous state or activity extending to the reference time, thus failing to meet the criteria for DtUniv.

TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have come' is asserted as a realized fact within Jesus's direct speech, stating his actual purpose in the world, not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'have come' is a simple present perfect (auxiliary 'have' + past participle 'come') with no past morphology, future marking, modal auxiliary, or progressive aspect, thus it does not instantiate TLTS.

FullText: When his own people heard of this they went to apprehend him . They said : » He has lost his senses . «

FullText: » If Satan has risen up against himself , he is divided , cannot stand and will come to an end .

ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'rise up' denotes directed motion/change-of-location where the sole argument (Satan) undergoes displacement as an internal argument, with no external argument licensed, matching the unaccusative criteria.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has risen up' is unaccusative (the subject Satan undergoes a change of state rather than initiating an action), which does not instantiate ASUnrg as it lacks an external argument functioning as agent/initiator.

ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has risen up' contains no negation marker ('not'/'n't'), negative quantifier, 'never', or 'neither...nor' construction; the negation in the clause applies to subsequent predicates ('cannot stand'), not to the target gram itself.

DtUniv_explanation: The predicate 'has risen up' denotes a punctual, completed action (a single event of rising up) rather than a continuous state or habitual series extending to the reference time, and lacks duration adverbials indicating extension.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has risen up' is a transition event (rise up = change of state from not-risen to risen) construed as a completed whole, meeting the criterion for bounded perfective aspect.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has risen up' appears in the protasis of a conditional construction ('If Satan has risen up...'), which presents the situation as hypothetical rather than asserted as realized fact.

VcPass_explanation: The construction 'has risen up' is active voice (auxiliary 'have' + present participle 'risen'), not passive voice; passive voice requires 'have' + 'been' + past participle, and the subject 'Satan' is the semantic agent performing the action, not the patient.

FullText: » These are they by the wayside where the word is sown . When they have heard ; Satan comes and takes away the word that has been sown in them .

AktSemel_explanation: The event 'have heard' entails a resulting change of state (transition from not having heard to having heard) and does not represent a single punctual occurrence without consequent result, thus it is not semelfactive.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have heard' denotes a bounded perceptual event with a defined endpoint (the moment of hearing), not a state that holds over time without dynamic change or progression.
