ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'healed' is passive voice with an internal argument ('of evil spirits and infirmities'), making it unaccusative rather than unergative; the subject does not function as an external agent/initiator.
AktAcc_explanation: The event 'had been healed' denotes a gradual change-of-state from a condition of illness/affliction to a defined result state of wellness, culminating in a completed transformation that satisfies the accomplishment criterion.
AktAch_explanation: The past perfect passive 'had been healed' denotes a durative process of healing rather than a punctual transition; it describes an extended therapeutic process with internal duration, not a boundary-crossing moment.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'had been healed' denotes a bounded, resultative state with an inherent endpoint (transition from infirm to healed), not a dynamic durative activity that unfolds without completion.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'had been healed' entails a resulting change of state (from infirm to healed) and does not represent a single punctual dynamic occurrence without consequent result, thus it is not semelfactive.
AktState_explanation: The past perfect 'had been healed' denotes a bounded, completed dynamic event (healing as a process with an inherent endpoint) rather than a state that holds over time without change.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'who had been healed of evil spirits and infirmities' is a factual past description of women who actually experienced healing, not a counterfactual condition or unfulfilled wish contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had been healed' is a positive passive construction with no negation markers (not/n't), negative quantifiers, 'never', or 'neither...nor' structures present.
DtExp_explanation: The gram introduces a resulting state (the women being in a healed condition) that is relevant to the narrative context, not merely recording the occurrence of healing in the subject's life history.
DtRes_explanation: The construction 'had been healed' is a stative copula (past participle as adjective in copular form) that describes a state rather than encoding a transition; it does not indicate that a change of state is currently in effect at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a completed state change (healing from evil spirits) rather than a continuous situation extending to the reference time; there is no duration adverbial or indication that the healing process itself extends throughout a time span.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'had been healed' is a passive form asserting a bounded transition event (healing as a completed state-change), matching the set_to_1_if criterion for passive bounded transitions.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had been healed' is a past perfect passive construction asserting a realized fact in the actual world (the women were actually healed), not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'had been healed' contains the morphologically past auxiliary 'had' combined with 'been' followed by a past participle, satisfying two criteria: past auxiliary marking and the 'been' + participle structure, making it a past progressive perfect form rather than simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had been healed' instantiates VcPass: it has the form auxiliary 'had' + 'been' + past participle 'healed' of a transitive verb, the grammatical subject 'women' corresponds to the semantic patient (those affected by healing), and passive morphology is overt.

FullText: Traveling with him were the women who had been healed of evil spirits and infirmities . This included Mary Magdalene , from whom seven demons had gone out .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'had gone out' is intransitive with no overt direct object; it describes a one-participant event (the demons departing) without an external agent affecting an internal patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'gone out' denotes directed motion/change-of-location where the sole argument (seven demons) undergoes displacement as an internal argument with no external argument licensed, matching the unaccusative pattern.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'had gone out' denotes manner of motion with the subject 'seven demons' functioning as the external argument and no internal argument licensed, matching the positive criterion for manner of motion verbs.
AktAcc_explanation: The event 'had gone out' denotes a punctual boundary transition (demons departing/exiting) rather than a durative process culminating in a result state; it describes a single instantaneous change of location/state, not a gradual process directed toward completion.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'had gone out' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (demons departing/exiting), construable as a boundary event that can be modified by specific time-point adverbials and does not permit interruption of an ongoing process phase.
AktAct_explanation: The phrase 'had gone out' denotes a punctual, bounded event with an inherent endpoint (demons departing from Mary Magdalene), not a dynamic durative activity that unfolds without completion.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'had gone out' entails a resulting change of state (demons departing from Mary Magdalene), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status; it describes a completed action with consequent result, not a punctual occurrence without change of state.
AktState_explanation: The verb phrase 'had gone out' denotes a bounded, completed dynamic event (demons departing/exiting) with an inherent endpoint, not a state that holds over time without change.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'from whom seven demons had gone out' describes a past event that actually occurred (Mary Magdalene was healed), not a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had gone out' contains no negation marker (not/n't), negative quantifier, 'never', or 'neither...nor' construction; it presents the event as realized rather than absent.
DtExp_explanation: The negative Perfect 'had gone out' (demons departing from Mary Magdalene) functions as a claim about the occurrence of an event in her life history, focusing on whether the exorcism happened rather than on a resulting state.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'had gone out' describes a change of state (demons departing from Mary Magdalene) where the resulting state—that the demons are no longer in her—persists at the reference time, matching the motion verb criterion where the mover has moved away and remains away.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'seven demons had gone out' describes a punctual, completed event (exorcism) rather than a continuous state or habitual series extending to the reference time, lacking duration adverbials or non-terminating situation markers.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'had gone out' construes a transition event (departure/exit of demons) as a completed whole, presenting it as a bounded occurrence rather than an ongoing or habitual situation.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had gone out' is a past perfect form asserting a realized fact in the actual world (that demons departed from Mary Magdalene), not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'had gone out' is a past perfect form with morphologically past auxiliary 'had', which instantiates TLTS according to the set_to_1_if criterion for past perfect constructions.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had gone out' lacks the required passive morphology (auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle); it is an active construction with the intransitive verb 'go', where the subject 'seven demons' is the agent performing the action, not the semantic patient.

FullText: » Those by the side of the road are they who have heard . Then the devil comes and takes the word from their hearts that they may not believe and be saved .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have heard' lacks an overt direct object (the object of hearing is not explicitly stated in the clause), and therefore does not instantiate the two-participant transitive pattern required by ASTrns.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'heard' licenses an external argument (the implicit subject 'they' who perform the action of hearing), making it a transitive verb with an agent, not an unaccusative verb.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'have heard' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('they') functioning as an external argument (experiencer/agent of perception) with no internal argument (direct object) realized in the clause.
AktAcc_explanation: The event 'have heard' denotes a punctual transition into the perceptual state of awareness (hearing/understanding the word), not a durative process with a measurable duration leading to completion, and does not satisfy any of the set_to_1_if criteria.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have heard' denotes a punctual perceptual transition into awareness (hearing the word), which is a near-instantaneous crossing of a definable threshold from not-hearing to hearing, matching the positive example 'They have heard the news.'
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have heard' denotes a bounded perceptual event with a defined result state (the word has been received), not a dynamic durative activity that unfolds without an inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The phrase 'have heard' entails a resulting change of state (transition from not having heard to having heard) and does not represent a single punctual dynamic occurrence without consequent result, thus it is not semelfactive.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have heard' denotes a bounded perceptual event (the act of hearing/receiving the word), not a stative condition; it involves dynamic change and a defined endpoint, matching the negative example 'We heard the news.'
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'who have heard' is a factual relative clause describing people who actually heard the word, not a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have heard' is not negated; it is a positive assertion that those by the side of the road 'have heard' the word, with negation appearing only in the subsequent clause 'may not believe'.
DtExp_explanation: The negative Perfect construction 'have heard' (in context of those who have heard the word) focuses on the occurrence of a perceptual experience as part of the subject's life history, matching the set_to_1_if criteria for perception verbs and experiential aspect.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have heard' reports a past auditory event without indicating a persisting resultant state; the clause merely describes that people heard the word, not that a changed condition currently holds as a result.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'have heard' describes a punctual, completed action of receiving/perceiving the word, not a continuous state or habitual series extending to the reference time, and lacks duration adverbials or 'since' markers indicating universal aspect.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'have heard' presents a stative/experiential situation (hearing/receiving information) without construing a bounded transition or completion; it describes a state of having received the word rather than a discrete bounded event with a construed endpoint.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have heard' is a present perfect form asserting a realized state in the actual world (those people have actually heard the word), not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'have heard' is a simple present perfect form with no past auxiliary, future marking, modal auxiliary, or progressive 'been' + participle construction, so it does not instantiate TLTS.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'have heard' lacks the required passive morphology (auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle); it is an active perfect construction where the grammatical subject 'they' is the semantic agent performing the hearing action.

FullText: When he arrived on the shore , a demon-possessed man met him . He wore no clothes and did not live in a house but in the tombs . He had been this way a long time .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'had been' is a copular/stative verb with no overt direct object; 'this way' is an adverbial phrase describing manner/state, not an internal argument affected by an external argument.
ASUncc_explanation: The copular 'be' clause 'had been this way' has an external argument (implicit 'he') functioning as the subject, and 'this way' is a predicative complement describing a state/condition rather than the sole internal argument undergoing change, so it does not meet the unaccusative criteria.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'had been' is a copular/stative verb that takes an internal argument (the predicate adjective 'this way'), not an unergative verb with only an external argument and no internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The phrase 'had been this way' describes a stative condition (existing in a particular state/manner) rather than a durative process directed toward a defined endpoint; it lacks the inherent culmination structure required for accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The phrase 'had been this way' denotes a durative state of existing in a particular condition over an extended period ('a long time'), not a punctual transition into a new state, and does not permit modification by a specific time-point adverbial.
AktAct_explanation: The phrase 'had been this way' denotes a dynamic, durative situation of existing in a particular state/condition over an extended period ('a long time'), which is homogeneous and can be naturally modified by durative adverbials without implying a defined endpoint or goal.
AktSemel_explanation: The phrase 'had been this way' describes a state or condition persisting over an extended duration ('a long time'), not a single punctual dynamic occurrence, thus it is durative rather than semelfactive.
AktState_explanation: The phrase 'had been this way' denotes a persistent state or condition that held over an extended duration ('a long time') without internal dynamic change or progression, meeting the criteria for AktState.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'He had been this way a long time' is a factual past statement describing a state that actually occurred, not a counterfactual condition or unfulfilled wish.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had been this way' contains no negation markers (not, n't, never) and no negative quantifiers; it presents the state as realized and occurring in reality.
DtExp_explanation: The gram 'had been this way' introduces a persistent result state (the man's condition of being in this state for a long time) relevant to the narrative context, not an experiential entry in the subject's life history.
DtRes_explanation: The construction 'had been this way' is a stative copula describing a persistent condition rather than encoding a transition from one state to another; it reports a durative situation without a change of state.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'He had been this way a long time' contains the duration adverbial 'a long time' and denotes a non-terminating state (being in a certain condition) that extended continuously from a past point to the reference time of the narrative.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'had been this way a long time' presents an unbounded, ongoing state or habitual condition rather than a single bounded occurrence with a construed endpoint; the adverbial 'a long time' emphasizes duration and continuity, not completion.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had been this way' is a past perfect construction asserting a realized state of affairs in the actual world (the man's condition over a long time), not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past, making this a past perfect form rather than a simple present perfect, which satisfies the first set_to_1_if condition.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'had been this way' is a copular structure with 'been' as a linking verb followed by an adverbial phrase ('this way'), not the required passive morphology of 'have' + 'been' + past participle of a transitive verb.

FullText: Jesus asked him : » What is your name ? « He answered : » Legion « for many demons had entered into him .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'had entered into' has an overt external argument ('many demons') and an overt internal argument ('him'), satisfying the requirement for a two-participant transitive event directed from agent toward patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'enter' licenses an external argument (the demons as agent performing the action of entering), so it does not meet the unaccusative criterion of having no external argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'entered into' licenses an internal argument ('him'), making it transitive and thus not an unergative one-participant event with only an external argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The event 'had entered into him' denotes a punctual boundary transition (demons crossing a threshold into the person) rather than a durative process with a measurable duration leading to completion; it lacks the inherent process phase characteristic of accomplishments.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'had entered into' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (demons crossing the threshold into the man), is not interruptible, and represents a definable boundary event rather than an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: The phrase 'had entered into him' denotes a punctual, bounded event with an inherent endpoint (the completion of entering), not a dynamic durative activity that unfolds without a defined goal.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had entered into him' entails a resulting change of state (demons being inside him), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status according to the CRIs.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'had entered into' denotes a dynamic, bounded event of demons moving into him, involving internal change and progression, not a static state that holds uniformly over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'many demons had entered into him' is a factual past statement explaining why the man's name is 'Legion'; it describes an event that occurred in the narrative world, not a counterfactual condition or unfulfilled wish.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had entered into' contains no negation marker ('not', 'n't'), negative quantifier, 'never', or 'neither...nor' construction; the clause presents the event as realized rather than absent.
DtExp_explanation: The clause 'many demons had entered into him' introduces a resulting state (demons being inside him) that is relevant to the narrative context explaining his condition, rather than focusing on the occurrence of the event as a life-history entry.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'had entered into' describes a change of possession/state (demons entering a person) where the resulting state—that the demons are now inside him—persists at the reference time, as evidenced by the present-tense context explaining his condition.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'many demons had entered into him' describes a punctual, completed action of entering (a transition into a new state) rather than a continuous state or activity extending to the reference time, and lacks duration adverbials indicating extension.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'had entered into' construes a transition event (entering) as a completed, bounded whole with a clear endpoint, fitting the perfective aspect profile.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had entered into' is a past perfect form asserting a realized fact in the actual world (the demons had actually entered the man), not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past, making this a past perfect form rather than a simple present perfect, which satisfies the first set_to_1_if condition.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had entered into' lacks the required passive morphology (auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle); it is an active voice construction where 'demons' is the grammatical subject and semantic agent.

FullText: They went out to see what happened . They came to Jesus and found the man who had been demon possessed . He was clothed , in his right mind and sitting at the feet of Jesus . They were afraid .
ASTrns_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('had been possessed'), and the lexical verb 'possess' licenses both an external argument (demon/agent, implied in the 'by'-phrase sense) and an internal argument (the man, overt grammatical subject in passive construction).
ASUncc_explanation: The verb phrase 'had been demon possessed' is a passive construction where an external agent (demon/possessing force) acts upon the internal argument (the man), thus licensing an external argument and failing to meet the unaccusative criteria.
ASUnrg_explanation: The target 'had been demon possessed' is a passive construction where the subject 'the man' functions as an internal argument (patient/theme) undergoing a state, not as an external argument (agent/initiator) of a one-participant event.
AktAcc_explanation: The target describes a state condition (being possessed) rather than a durative process culminating in a defined endpoint; the past perfect 'had been possessed' marks entry into a state, not a gradual change-of-state with inherent completion.
AktAch_explanation: The past perfect 'had been demon possessed' denotes a durative state or condition rather than a punctual transition; it describes an extended condition of possession, not a boundary-crossing moment of entering that state.
AktAct_explanation: The target describes a bounded result state ('had been demon possessed') indicating a completed condition with a defined endpoint, not a dynamic durative activity that unfolds without inherent completion.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'had been demon possessed' describes a resulting state or condition (change of state from possessed to no longer possessed), not a single punctual dynamic occurrence without consequent result, so it does not instantiate AktSemel.
AktState_explanation: The past perfect 'had been demon possessed' denotes a bounded dynamic event (the process of becoming possessed) with an inherent endpoint, not a stative situation holding uniformly over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'who had been demon possessed' describes a factual past state that actually occurred; it is not counterfactual, as the man was indeed possessed before being healed, and the text presents this as a real event that happened.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had been demon possessed' is a positive assertion describing a past state; there is no negation marker ('not'/'n't'), negative quantifier, 'never', or 'neither...nor' construction present.
DtExp_explanation: The clause introduces a result state (the man being in a condition of demon possession) that is relevant for the subsequent narrative description of his current state ('clothed, in his right mind'), making it non-experiential per the edge case rule.
DtRes_explanation: The construction 'had been demon possessed' is a stative copula describing a prior condition rather than encoding a transition with a persisting result state; the context indicates the man is no longer possessed (he is now 'in his right mind'), so no resulting state from the possession currently holds.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a past state that has terminated by the reference time (the man is now 'in his right mind'), indicating a transition into a new state rather than a continuous situation extending to the reference time.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'had been demon possessed' uses the passive form 'had been' + past participle and describes a bounded prior state/condition that is now resolved (the man is no longer possessed, as evidenced by being 'in his right mind'), presenting the possession as a completed, bounded situation rather than an ongoing process.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The past perfect 'had been demon possessed' asserts a realized state of affairs in the actual world—a fact about the man's prior condition—not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past, making this a past perfect form rather than a simple present perfect, which satisfies the first set_to_1_if condition.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had been demon possessed' instantiates passive voice morphology (auxiliary 'had' + 'been' + past participle 'possessed'), with the grammatical subject 'the man' functioning as the semantic patient (the entity affected by possession), meeting all criteria for VcPass.

FullText: » Return to your house and declare the great things God has done for you . « He went throughout the whole city telling the great things Jesus had done for him .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has done' lacks an overt direct object (the prepositional phrase 'for you' is an oblique argument, not a direct object), failing to meet the requirement of an overt grammatical subject and overt direct object.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'do' licenses an external argument (God as agent performing actions), making it transitive rather than unaccusative; the sole argument does not function as an internal theme/patient.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'done' licenses an internal argument ('the great things'), making it transitive rather than a one-participant unergative event.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'has done' denotes a punctual, completed action without an inherent durative process phase or gradual change-of-state leading to a defined endpoint; it marks a boundary transition into a completed state rather than an accomplishment with measurable duration.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has done' denotes a completed action with inherent process phases (the accomplishment of multiple unspecified 'great things'), not a punctual transition into a new state, and does not meet the criteria for achievement aspect.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has done' denotes a completed action with a defined result state (great things accomplished), not a dynamic durative situation without inherent endpoint, and cannot be naturally modified by 'for X time' without changing meaning.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has done' refers to multiple great things accomplished by God, construed as a durative accomplishment with resulting change of state rather than a single punctual occurrence without consequence.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has done' denotes a completed dynamic action with internal progression and a bounded endpoint, not a stative situation that holds uniformly over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The target gram describes actual events that have occurred (God's past actions), not a counterfactual situation contrary to fact; it is a factual past statement without conditional structure, unfulfilled wishes, or explicit contrary-to-fact marking.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has done' is not negated by 'not'/'n't', does not contain a negative quantifier, is not modified by 'never', and does not appear in a 'neither…nor' construction; it presents the event as realized rather than absent.
DtExp_explanation: The Perfect 'has done' focuses on the occurrence of God's actions as part of the subject's life history/experience rather than on a resulting state, and the parallel structure with 'had done for him' (which the person goes throughout the city telling about) emphasizes the experiential aspect of what God has accomplished.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has done' reports past actions/miracles without entailing a persisting resultant state; it is a narrative report of completed events rather than a change of state that holds at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The predicate 'has done' denotes a completed action with a resultant state rather than a continuous activity extending to the reference time; it lacks duration adverbials and describes discrete accomplishments, not a non-terminating situation.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has done' presents God's actions as a bounded whole with a construed endpoint, construing completed events as discrete accomplishments rather than ongoing or habitual situations.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The target 'has done' is a present perfect form asserting realized actions in the actual world (the great things God has accomplished), not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'has done' is a simple present perfect (auxiliary 'has' + past participle 'done') with no past morphology, future marking, modal auxiliary, or progressive aspect, so it does not instantiate TLTS.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has done' is active voice (agent 'God' as grammatical subject, no auxiliary 'been' + past participle structure), not passive voice.

FullText: » Return to your house and declare the great things God has done for you . « He went throughout the whole city telling the great things Jesus had done for him .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'had done' has an overt grammatical subject (Jesus) and an overt internal argument ('the great things'), satisfying the requirement for a two-participant transitive construction with both external and internal arguments.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'do' licenses an external argument (Jesus as agent) and an internal argument (the great things as theme), making it a transitive verb that does not meet the unaccusative criteria.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'had done' licenses an internal argument ('the great things'), making it transitive and thus not an unergative one-participant event.
AktAcc_explanation: The event 'had done for him' denotes a durative process of performing miracles or acts of healing that culminated in a defined result state (the great things accomplished), fitting the accomplishment profile of directed action toward a goal with inherent completion.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'had done' denotes a completed action with inherent process phases (miracles, healings, teachings) rather than a punctual transition into a new state, and does not naturally modify with specific time-point adverbials in this context.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'had done' denotes a completed action with a defined result state (the great things that Jesus accomplished), not a dynamic durative situation without an inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had done' refers to multiple great things accomplished by Jesus (a complex of actions with resulting changes of state), not a single punctual occurrence without consequent result, so it does not instantiate the semelfactive feature.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'had done' denotes a completed dynamic event (miracles/actions performed by Jesus) with internal progression and a bounded endpoint, not a stative situation holding over time without change.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'Jesus had done for him' is a factual past statement describing events that actually occurred, not a counterfactual condition or unfulfilled wish.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had done' is not negated by 'not'/'n't', does not contain a negative quantifier, is not modified by 'never', and does not appear in a 'neither…nor' construction; it presents the event as realized rather than absent.
DtExp_explanation: The clause 'Jesus had done for him' introduces a resulting state of miraculous deeds/benefits that persist and are relevant for the subsequent narrative action (the man's declaration throughout the city), making it non-experiential rather than a life-history entry.
DtRes_explanation: The clause 'Jesus had done for him' reports a past interaction (miraculous healing) without entailing a persisting resultative state; it is embedded in a narrative context describing what the man was telling others, not what currently holds as a result.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'Jesus had done for him' describes discrete, completed actions (miracles/great things) rather than a continuous state or habitual series extending to the reference time, lacking duration adverbials and denoting bounded events.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The past perfect 'had done' presents Jesus's actions as a bounded, completed whole that serves as a discrete referent in the narrative ('the great things Jesus had done'), construed as a single accomplished event rather than an ongoing or habitual situation.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had done' is a past perfect form asserting a realized fact about Jesus's actions in the actual world within the narrative, not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past, making this a past perfect form rather than a simple present perfect, which satisfies the first set_to_1_if condition.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had done' is active voice (simple past perfect of transitive verb 'do' with object 'great things'), not passive voice; passive voice requires the auxiliary sequence 'have' + 'been' + past participle.

FullText: He said to her : » Daughter , your faith has made you whole ; go in peace . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has made' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('your faith') and an overt direct object ('you'), satisfying the first condition for ASTrns.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'made' licenses an external argument (your faith) that acts as an agent/causer bringing about a change of state in the internal argument (you), so it does not meet the unaccusative criteria which requires no external argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'made' is transitive and licenses an internal argument (direct object 'you'), so it does not instantiate ASUnrg which requires no internal argument.
AktAcc_explanation: The event 'has made you whole' denotes a punctual transition into a result state (wholeness/healing) rather than a durative process unfolding toward completion; it cannot be naturally modified by 'in X time' adverbials and represents an instantaneous causative achievement of a state change.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has made (you) whole' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (from afflicted/broken to whole/healed), represents a boundary point of state-change rather than an unfolding process, and cannot be interrupted mid-occurrence, satisfying the criteria for AktAch.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has made (you) whole' denotes a resultative, punctual event with an inherent endpoint and defined result state (wholeness achieved), not a dynamic durative activity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'has made you whole' entails a resulting change of state (from not whole to whole), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status according to the CRI requirement that semelfactives must not entail a resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has made' denotes a dynamic, bounded event of transformation (causing wholeness) with an inherent endpoint, not a stative situation that holds uniformly over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'your faith has made you whole' describes a factual past event that has actually occurred, not a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has made' is not negated by 'not'/'n't', does not contain a negative quantifier, is not modified by 'never', and does not appear in a 'neither…nor' construction; the event is presented as realized rather than absent.
DtExp_explanation: The perfect 'has made you whole' introduces a resulting state (the subject being healed/whole) that is relevant to the subsequent discourse ('go in peace'), making it non-experiential rather than a life-history entry.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has made' describes a change of state (from not whole to whole), and the context indicates the resulting state (being whole/healed) persists at the reference time, as evidenced by the directive 'go in peace' which presupposes the healing is complete and current.
DtUniv_explanation: The predicate 'has made you whole' denotes a punctual, resultative action (transition into a new state of wholeness) rather than a continuous state or habitual series extending to the reference time, and lacks duration adverbials indicating extension.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has made you whole' is a transition event (change of state from not-whole to whole) construed as a completed bounded occurrence with a clear endpoint, fitting the perfective aspect.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has made' is a present perfect indicative asserting a realized fact in the actual world (your faith has accomplished the state of being whole), not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.

FullText: Her parents were amazed . He charged them to tell no man what had been done .
ASTrns_explanation: The target 'had been done' is a passive construction that licenses both an external argument (agent, unexpressed) and an internal argument (patient, implied by the context of the preceding clause), satisfying the passive condition in set_to_1_if.
ASUncc_explanation: The target 'had been done' is a passive construction where an external agent (implied but not expressed) performs the action on the internal argument, thus licensing an external argument and failing to meet the unaccusative criteria.
ASUnrg_explanation: The target verb 'had been done' is a passive construction where the logical object (what was done) is promoted to subject position, and the logical agent is absent or demoted, failing to meet the requirement that the subject function as an external argument (agent/initiator) with no internal argument licensed.
AktAcc_explanation: The past perfect passive 'had been done' describes a punctual boundary transition into a completed state rather than a durative process with an inherent temporal unfolding toward a goal; the event is construed as a single transition point, not a measurable process phase leading to completion.
AktAch_explanation: The past perfect passive 'had been done' denotes a completed durative process rather than a punctual transition; it refers to an extended action or series of actions that culminated in a result state, lacking the instantaneous boundary-crossing quality required for AktAch.
AktAct_explanation: The passive construction 'had been done' denotes a completed action with a defined result state, lacking the dynamic, durative, ongoing character required for AktAct; it describes a bounded event with an inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'had been done' is a perfect passive construction that entails a resulting change of state (something was accomplished/completed), not a punctual dynamic occurrence without consequent result, so it does not instantiate AktSemel.
AktState_explanation: The verb phrase 'had been done' denotes a completed dynamic event with an inherent endpoint, not a state that holds over time without change; it describes a bounded past action rather than a stative situation.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'what had been done' is a factual past event (a miracle that actually occurred) embedded in an indirect command, not a counterfactual conditional protasis, apodosis, unfulfilled wish, or inverted conditional structure.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had been done' is not negated by 'not'/'n't', does not contain a negative quantifier in subject or object position, is not modified by 'never', and does not appear in a 'neither…nor' construction; the negation ('no man') applies to the indirect object, not to the target gram itself.
DtExp_explanation: The past perfect 'had been done' introduces a resulting state (a miraculous deed that occurred) relevant to the narrative progression—the parents are charged not to tell about it—rather than presenting the event as part of a subject's life history.
DtRes_explanation: The construction 'had been done' is a stative copula (past perfect passive of 'be') that describes a state rather than encoding a transition; it reports a past condition without indicating a persisting resultative state at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'what had been done' describes a completed punctual action (a discrete event) rather than a continuous state or habitual series extending to the reference time, lacking duration adverbials or iterative markers required for DtUniv classification.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'what had been done' presents a passive bounded transition event (the completion of an action) as a single whole, matching the form 'have been' + past participle asserting a bounded event rather than an ongoing process.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had been done' is a past perfect form asserting a realized fact (the action of doing something) within the actual narrative world, not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past, and 'been' is followed by the past participle 'done' in a progressive-like construction, satisfying the first and fourth set_to_1_if conditions for past perfect and perfect progressive forms.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had been done' instantiates VcPass: it has the form auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle of a transitive verb, the grammatical subject 'what' corresponds to the semantic patient (the entity affected by the event), and overt passive morphology is present.

FullText: Others said that Elijah had appeared and others and that one of the old prophets had risen again .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'had appeared' is intransitive with no overt direct object; it takes only an external argument (Elijah) and does not license an internal argument, failing all set_to_1_if conditions.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'appeared' denotes appearance/coming-into-being without licensing an external argument; the sole argument (Elijah) functions as an internal argument (theme) undergoing the state of appearing, matching the unaccusative criterion.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'appeared' is an unaccusative verb denoting appearance/coming-into-being where the subject functions as an internal argument (theme), not an external argument (agent/initiator), thus failing the ASUnrg criteria.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'appeared' denotes a punctual transition into visibility or presence without a durative process phase leading to a defined endpoint; it marks a boundary event rather than a gradual change-of-state or directed action toward completion.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'appeared' denotes a punctual perceptual/cognitive transition into visibility or awareness (crossing the threshold from unseen to seen), naturally modifiable by time-point adverbials (e.g., 'appeared at noon'), and lacks measurable internal duration, satisfying multiple set_to_1_if criteria.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'appeared' denotes a punctual, bounded perceptual event with an inherent endpoint (the moment of appearance), not a dynamic durative situation, and does not allow natural modification by 'for X time' adverbials without semantic oddity.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had appeared' entails a resulting change of state (Elijah transitioning from not being present to being present), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status according to the CRIs.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'had appeared' denotes a bounded dynamic event of coming into view or manifestation, which involves eventive unfolding and change rather than a static state holding over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'had appeared' is a reported past event in indirect discourse ('Others said that...'), not a counterfactual conditional protasis, apodosis, unfulfilled wish, or inverted conditional structure.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had appeared' is not negated by 'not'/'n't', does not contain a negative quantifier, is not modified by 'never', and does not appear in a 'neither…nor' construction; the event is presented as realized rather than absent.
DtExp_explanation: The negative Perfect 'had appeared' presents the event as part of the subject's (Elijah's) life history, answering whether the event occurred at least once without asserting a resulting state, fitting the experiential perfect category.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'had appeared' describes a change of state (transition from not-present to present) and the context indicates the resulting state (Elijah's presence) persists at the reference time of the reported speech.
DtUniv_explanation: The predicate 'had appeared' denotes a punctual, discrete event (appearance) rather than a continuous state or habitual series extending to the reference time, and no duration adverbials are present.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'had appeared' is a transition event (appearance as a bounded occurrence) construed as a completed whole in past narrative sequence, meeting the criterion for perfective aspect.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had appeared' is embedded in a reported speech context ('Others said that...') and asserts a realized event within the narrative world, not a projected or hypothetical situation.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past, making this a past perfect form rather than a simple present perfect, which satisfies the first set_to_1_if condition.
VcPass_explanation: The form 'had appeared' is auxiliary 'had' + past participle, not the passive sequence 'had been' + past participle; 'appeared' is an intransitive verb with no semantic patient as grammatical subject, making this active voice, not passive voice.

FullText: Others said that Elijah had appeared and others and that one of the old prophets had risen again .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'had risen' is intransitive with no overt direct object or internal argument; it describes a one-participant event (the prophet rising) without an external agent affecting an internal patient.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'had risen' denotes directed motion/change-of-location (rising from the dead) where the sole argument 'one of the old prophets' functions as an internal argument undergoing displacement, with no external argument licensed.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'had risen' is unaccusative (subject is an internal argument undergoing a change of state), not an unergative one-participant event with an external argument agent/initiator.
AktAcc_explanation: The event 'had risen' denotes a punctual transition from a state of being dead to a state of being alive/resurrected, marking a single boundary crossing rather than a durative process with a measurable duration leading to completion.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'had risen' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (from dead to alive/resurrected), construable as a boundary crossing that does not permit interruption and lacks measurable internal duration.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'had risen' denotes a punctual change of state with an inherent endpoint (transition from dead to alive), not a dynamic durative activity that unfolds without a defined result state.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had risen' entails a resulting change of state (from dead to alive/present), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status according to the CRI requirement that semelfactives must not entail a resulting change of state.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'had risen' denotes a dynamic change of state (movement from a lower to higher position or from death to life), involving internal progression and eventive unfolding, which does not satisfy the stative criteria.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'one of the old prophets had risen again' is a reported statement (indirect discourse following 'said that') expressing what others believed or said, not a counterfactual condition or unfulfilled wish; it describes a past event within reported speech without markers of contrary-to-fact meaning.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had risen' contains no negation marker (not/n't), is not modified by 'never', is not in a negative quantifier construction, and does not appear in a 'neither...nor' structure; the event is presented as realized rather than absent.
DtExp_explanation: The clause 'one of the old prophets had risen again' presents a narrative event that introduces a resulting state (the prophet being alive/present again) relevant to the discourse context, not an experiential entry in a life history.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'had risen' describes a change of state (from dead to alive) for 'one of the old prophets,' and the resulting state (being alive/present) persists at the reference time of the narrative.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'one of the old prophets had risen again' describes a punctual, completed event (resurrection) rather than a continuous state or activity extending to the reference time, lacking duration adverbials or non-terminating situation markers required for DtUniv.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'had risen' is a transition event (rising from death) construed as a completed whole, presenting the situation as a single bounded occurrence with a construed endpoint.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had risen' is embedded in a reported speech context ('Others said that...') and asserts a realized state of affairs within the narrative world, not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past, making this a past perfect form rather than a simple present perfect, which satisfies the first set_to_1_if condition.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had risen' is an active voice construction (auxiliary 'had' + past participle of intransitive verb 'rise'), not the passive morphology 'have/had + been + past participle' required for VcPass; the subject 'one of the old prophets' is the semantic agent/experiencer, not the patient.

FullText: When the apostles returned they told Jesus about the things they had done . They traveled to the city of Bethsaida .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'had done' has an overt grammatical subject ('they') and an overt direct object ('the things'), satisfying the first condition for ASTrns marking.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'do' licenses an external argument (the apostles as agent performing actions), so it does not meet the unaccusative criterion of having only an internal argument with no external argument licensed.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'done' is a past participle of 'do' functioning as the lexical verb in 'had done', which is a transitive verb that licenses an internal argument (the things they had done), making it not an unergative one-participant event.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'had done' refers to completed actions without specifying a durative process directed toward a defined endpoint; it denotes punctual accomplishment of unspecified tasks rather than a single bounded event with inherent temporal development.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'had done' denotes a completed durative activity or process (the apostles' missionary work) rather than a punctual transition into a new state; it lacks the instantaneous boundary-crossing quality required for AktAch classification.
AktAct_explanation: The phrase 'had done' denotes a completed action with an inherent endpoint and result state, lacking the dynamic, durative, homogeneous quality required for AktAct classification.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'had done' is a perfect aspect construction referring to completed actions with resulting states (the apostles' missionary activities and their outcomes), not a single punctual dynamic occurrence without change of state.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'had done' denotes a completed dynamic event with internal progression and an inherent endpoint, not a state that holds over time without change.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'they had done' is a factual past perfect describing events that actually occurred (the apostles' completed actions), not a counterfactual situation contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had done' is not negated by 'not'/'n't', does not contain a negative quantifier, is not modified by 'never', and does not appear in a 'neither…nor' construction; the event is presented as realized rather than absent.
DtExp_explanation: The clause 'they had done' introduces a resulting state (completed missionary activities) that is relevant for the narrative progression—the apostles report their accomplishments to Jesus, making the result state central to the discourse rather than focusing on the occurrence as a life-history entry.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'had done' describes a completed change of state (the apostles transitioned from not having done things to having done them), and the context indicates this resulting state of completion persists at the reference time when they report it to Jesus.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'they had done' describes discrete, completed actions (things the apostles accomplished during their mission) rather than a continuous state or habitual series extending to the reference time, and lacks duration adverbials indicating extension.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The past perfect 'had done' presents the apostles' actions as a bounded, completed whole that occurred prior to the narrative moment when they reported to Jesus, construing the situation as a single accomplished event rather than an ongoing or habitual activity.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had done' is a past perfect form asserting a realized fact in the actual world (the apostles actually performed actions), not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past, making this a past perfect form rather than a simple present perfect, which satisfies the first set_to_1_if condition.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had done' is active voice (auxiliary 'had' + past participle 'done'), not passive voice; the grammatical subject 'they' is the semantic agent performing the action, not the patient undergoing it.

FullText: They said : » John the Baptist . Others say Elijah and still others say one of the old prophets has risen again . «
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'has risen' is intransitive with no overt direct object or internal argument; it describes a one-participant event (the subject undergoing a change of state) rather than a two-participant transitive event.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'has risen' denotes a change-of-location/directed motion event where the sole argument (one of the old prophets) undergoes upward displacement as an internal argument, with no external argument licensed.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'has risen' is unaccusative (one of the prophets undergoes a change of state/position), not unergative; the subject is an internal argument that moves into external position, not an external argument/agent initiating an activity.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'has risen' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (from dead to alive/resurrected) rather than a durative process with an inherent culmination phase, making it an achievement rather than an accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'has risen' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (from dead to alive/resurrected), construed as a boundary crossing rather than an unfolding process, and naturally permits time-point modification (e.g., 'has risen at dawn'), meeting the criteria for achievement aspect.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'has risen' denotes a change of state with an inherent endpoint (transition from dead to alive) and a defined result state, not a dynamic durative activity that unfolds without completion.
AktSemel_explanation: The verb 'has risen' entails a resulting change of state (from dead to alive/resurrected) and is not a punctual dynamic occurrence without consequent result, thus it does not instantiate AktSemel.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'has risen' denotes a dynamic change of state (movement from dead to alive/resurrected) with an inherent endpoint and internal progression, not a static situation that holds uniformly over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'one of the old prophets has risen again' is an open possibility or reported belief within indirect speech, not a counterfactual condition; it describes what people say might be the case, not a situation understood not to have occurred.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'has risen' is not negated by 'not'/'n't', does not contain a negative quantifier in subject or object position, is not modified by 'never', and does not appear in a 'neither…nor' construction; it is a positive assertion within reported speech.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'has risen' introduces a resulting state (the prophet is now alive/present) that is relevant for the narrative discourse, not merely an entry in life history.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'has risen' describes a motion/change of state where the subject (one of the old prophets) has moved from a prior state (dead) to a new state (alive/risen) that persists at the reference time, matching the resultative pattern of motion verbs with a destination state that remains in effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'one of the old prophets has risen again' describes a punctual event (resurrection) with a resultant state, not a continuous activity or state extending from a past point to the reference time, and lacks duration adverbials indicating extension.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'has risen' is a transition event (rising from death) construed as a completed whole, presenting the situation as a single bounded occurrence with a construed endpoint.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'has risen' appears in reported speech ('Others say...one of the old prophets has risen again') expressing what people claim or believe rather than what is asserted as fact by the narrator, placing it in a domain of reported opinion rather than realized fact.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'has risen' is a simple present perfect (auxiliary 'has' + past participle 'risen') with no past morphology, future marking, modal auxiliary, or progressive aspect, so it does not instantiate TLTS.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'has risen' lacks the required passive morphology (auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle); 'risen' is the past participle of an intransitive verb, and the subject 'one of the old prophets' is the semantic agent/experiencer, not the patient.

FullText: When the voice came Jesus was found alone . They kept quiet and told no man what they had seen .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'had seen' has an overt grammatical subject (they, implicit in 'what they had seen') and an overt direct object ('what'), satisfying the requirement for a two-participant transitive construction.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'see' licenses an external argument (the implicit subject 'they'), making it transitive rather than unaccusative; the sole overt argument 'what' functions as an internal argument (theme), but the presence of an external argument disqualifies it from ASUncc.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'had seen' licenses an internal argument (the direct object 'what'), making it transitive rather than a one-participant unergative event.
AktAcc_explanation: The verb 'had seen' denotes a punctual perceptual event—a single instantaneous transition into visual awareness—rather than a durative process culminating in a defined result state, and cannot be naturally modified by 'in X time' adverbials.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'had seen' denotes a punctual perceptual transition into awareness (perceiving/witnessing an event), which is a boundary transition into a new cognitive state, matching the achievement criterion for perceptual verbs.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'had seen' denotes a bounded perceptual event with a defined result state (having perceived something), not a dynamic durative activity; it cannot be naturally modified by 'for X time' without changing meaning, and it lacks the homogeneous property required for AktAct.
AktSemel_explanation: The past perfect 'had seen' entails a resulting change of state (knowledge/perception acquired) and does not construe a single punctual occurrence, thus it is not semelfactive.
AktState_explanation: The past perfect 'had seen' denotes a bounded perceptual event with internal dynamic development and a defined endpoint, not a stative situation holding over time.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'what they had seen' is a factual past event (they actually witnessed something) embedded in a reported speech context, not a counterfactual conditional protasis, apodosis, unfulfilled wish, or inverted conditional.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had seen' is not negated by 'not'/'n't', does not contain a negative quantifier in subject or object position, is not modified by 'never', and does not appear in a 'neither…nor' construction; the negation ('told no man') applies to the matrix verb 'told' rather than to the target gram.
DtExp_explanation: The verb 'had seen' is a perception verb focusing on the occurrence of an experience (witnessing an event) in the subject's life history rather than on a resulting state, matching the positive example 'We have seen the Eiffel Tower.'
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'had seen' reports a past perceptual event without indicating that a resulting state persists at the reference time; it merely describes what they witnessed, not a change of state currently in effect.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'what they had seen' describes a discrete, completed perceptual event (witnessing something) rather than a continuous state or habitual series extending to the reference time, lacking duration adverbials or non-terminating situation markers.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The predicate 'had seen' is a transition event (the act of perceiving/witnessing) construed as a completed whole within the narrative sequence, functioning as a discrete bounded occurrence in the past.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had seen' is a past perfect form asserting a realized fact in the actual world (what they witnessed), not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past, making this a past perfect form rather than a simple present perfect, which satisfies the first set_to_1_if condition.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'had seen' is active voice (auxiliary 'had' + past participle 'seen' of a transitive verb), not passive voice; the grammatical subject 'they' is the semantic agent performing the action of seeing, not the patient being affected.

FullText: » Woe to you Chorazin ! Woe to you Bethsaida ! If the mighty works had been done in Tyre and Sidon , which were done in you , they would have repented long ago , sitting in sackcloth and ashes .
ASTrns_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive with the lexical verb 'done' licensing both an external argument (implicit agent performing the works) and an internal argument (the overt direct object 'mighty works'), satisfying the passive condition in set_to_1_if.
ASUncc_explanation: This is a passive construction ('had been done') where an external argument (the implicit agent performing the works) is present but suppressed, not an unaccusative verb that fails to license an external argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'done' is passive voice with an implicit external agent and licenses an internal argument (the subject 'mighty works' is the patient/theme), failing the requirement for no internal argument and external argument function.
AktAcc_explanation: The event 'mighty works had been done' is a punctual accomplishment of discrete miraculous acts that constitute instantaneous transitions into completion, lacking an inherent durative process phase that unfolds toward a defined endpoint.
AktAch_explanation: The passive construction 'had been done' denotes a punctual transition into a completed state of performance (the mighty works coming into existence/occurrence), which is a change-of-state event construable as a boundary point rather than an unfolding process.
AktAct_explanation: The passive construction 'had been done' denotes a bounded, punctual event with an inherent endpoint (completion of the works), not a dynamic durative activity that unfolds without a defined result state.
AktSemel_explanation: The event 'had been done' refers to multiple miraculous works (plural 'works') construed as a durative or iterative series of accomplishments with resulting change of state, not a single punctual occurrence without consequent result.
AktState_explanation: The verb phrase 'had been done' denotes a bounded, completed dynamic event (the performance of mighty works), not a state that holds over time without change or progression.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The target gram occurs in the protasis of a counterfactual conditional ('If the mighty works had been done in Tyre and Sidon...'), where the condition is understood not to have been satisfied, paired with the apodosis 'they would have repented long ago', marking the situation as contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'had been done' contains no negation marker ('not', 'n't'), negative quantifier, 'never', or 'neither...nor' construction; it appears in a counterfactual conditional but is not itself negated.
DtExp_explanation: The clause introduces a counterfactual conditional where the resulting state of the mighty works having occurred in Tyre and Sidon is relevant for the subsequent consequence (they would have repented), making this narrative/result-state focused rather than experiential.
DtRes_explanation: The construction 'had been done' is a stative copula (past perfect passive) describing a state rather than encoding a transition; it reports a hypothetical past condition without indicating a persisting resultant state at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause describes a counterfactual conditional (hypothetical situation) rather than a situation extending continuously from a past point to reference time; it lacks duration adverbials and denotes a discrete event, not a continuous or habitual state.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'had been done' is a passive form asserting a bounded transition event (the performance of mighty works as a completed whole), fitting the criterion for passive bounded transitions.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'had been done' appears in a counterfactual conditional construction ('If the mighty works had been done...they would have repented'), presenting a hypothetical, non-realized situation contrary to fact.
TLTS_explanation: The auxiliary 'had' is morphologically past, and 'been' is followed by the past participle 'done', making this a past perfect progressive form that encodes tense specification other than simple present perfect.
VcPass_explanation: The target instantiates VcPass: it has the form 'had been done' (auxiliary 'have' + 'been' + past participle of transitive verb 'do'), the grammatical subject 'mighty works' is the semantic patient (entity affected by the action), and the construction exhibits overt passive morphology.

FullText: » Woe to you Chorazin ! Woe to you Bethsaida ! If the mighty works had been done in Tyre and Sidon , which were done in you , they would have repented long ago , sitting in sackcloth and ashes .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'repented' is intransitive with no overt direct object; it licenses only an external argument (the subject 'they') and does not take an internal argument, failing to meet the two-participant transitive requirement.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'repent' licenses an external argument (the subject 'they'), making it a transitive or agentive verb that does not meet the unaccusative criteria of encoding a one-participant event with only an internal argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'repented' introduces an overt grammatical subject ('they') functioning as an external argument (agent engaged in a volitional activity) with no internal argument licensed, matching the criterion for ASUnrg.
AktAcc_explanation: The event 'repent' involves a durative process of internal change and transformation toward a defined result state (the condition of being repentant), and is naturally modified by 'long ago' indicating a bounded temporal span, meeting the criteria for accomplishment.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'repented' denotes a punctual cognitive/moral transition into a new state of contrition or changed disposition, which is a definable threshold event that cannot be interrupted mid-occurrence and naturally occurs at a temporal boundary.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'repent' denotes a change of state or mental reorientation with an inherent result state (moving from unrepentant to repentant), not a dynamic durative activity that unfolds without an endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: Repentance entails a resulting change of state (from unrepentant to repentant condition) and is construed as a durative process rather than a single punctual occurrence, thus not semelfactive.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'would have repented' denotes a bounded, dynamic event of changing one's mind/behavior, not a state that holds over time without change; repentance is an eventive action with internal progression and an inherent endpoint.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The gram 'would have repented' occurs in the apodosis (result clause) of a counterfactual conditional introduced by 'If the mighty works had been done in Tyre and Sidon', expressing an unfulfilled past event contrary to fact.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'would have repented' contains no negation markers ('not', 'n't', 'never') and no negative quantifiers; it expresses a positive counterfactual event that would have occurred under different conditions.
DtExp_explanation: The conditional perfect 'would have repented' presents a hypothetical result state (the condition of having repented) rather than an experiential entry in the subject's life history, and the adverbial 'long ago' indicates a specific past time point rather than frequency or life-history occurrence.
DtRes_explanation: The conditional counterfactual construction 'would have repented' describes a hypothetical past state that never occurred, not a change of state that has actually resulted and persists at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The target 'would have repented' is a counterfactual conditional expressing a hypothetical past event, not a continuous state or habitual series extending to the reference time, and lacks duration adverbials indicating extension.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The gram 'would have repented' presents repentance as a single bounded transition event (a change of state from not repenting to repenting) that would have been completed as a whole in the counterfactual conditional scenario, meeting the criterion for perfective aspect.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'would have repented' is a modal auxiliary construction (would + have + past participle) appearing in the consequent of a counterfactual conditional ('If the mighty works had been done...they would have repented'), presenting the situation as hypothetical and unrealized rather than asserted as fact.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'would have repented' includes a modal auxiliary 'would' preceding 'have', which matches the set_to_1_if criterion for modal perfect forms.
VcPass_explanation: The target 'would have repented' is active voice (conditional perfect of a transitive verb with the grammatical subject 'they' as the semantic agent), not passive voice, which requires the auxiliary sequence 'have' + 'been' + past participle.

FullText: » Behold , I have given you authority to tread upon serpents and scorpions , and over all the power of the enemy : and nothing shall in any way hurt you .
ASTrns_explanation: The verb 'have given' introduces an overt grammatical subject (I), an overt direct object (you), and an overt internal argument (authority), satisfying the ditransitive pattern with both direct and indirect objects.
ASUncc_explanation: The verb 'give' licenses an external argument (the speaker as agent) and an internal argument (you as recipient/theme), making it a transitive verb that does not meet the unaccusative criteria of a one-participant event with only an internal argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'have given' is transitive and licenses an internal argument ('authority'), making it a two-participant event rather than a one-participant unergative event.
AktAcc_explanation: The event 'give authority' is a punctual transfer of a right or power that occurs at a single moment of completion without an inherent durative process phase leading toward the endpoint.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'have given' denotes a punctual transfer of authority into a new state, construable as a boundary transition that can occur at a specific moment (e.g., 'at that time I gave you authority'), lacks internal duration, and does not permit interruption of an ongoing process phase.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have given' denotes a completed transfer of authority with a defined result state (you now possess authority), not a dynamic durative situation without an inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'have given (you) authority' entails a resulting change of state (the recipient now possesses authority), which disqualifies it from semelfactive status; semelfactives require no consequent result condition.
AktState_explanation: The verb 'have given' denotes a completed transfer of authority (a bounded dynamic event with an inherent endpoint), not a state that holds uniformly over time without change.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'I have given you authority' is a factual statement of a completed action in the actual world, not a counterfactual condition or unfulfilled wish, and does not occur in a protasis, apodosis, or inverted conditional structure.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have given' is a positive assertion with no negation markers (not/n't), negative quantifiers, 'never', or 'neither...nor' constructions modifying it.
DtExp_explanation: The perfect 'have given' introduces a resulting state of authority that persists at reference time and is relevant for the subsequent clause describing what the recipient can do with that authority, making it non-experiential.
DtRes_explanation: The verb 'have given' describes a change of possession/status (granting authority), and the context 'I have given you authority to tread upon serpents and scorpions' entails that this authority is currently in effect and binding at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'I have given you authority' describes a completed transfer of authority (punctual result/transition into a new state) rather than a continuous state or activity extending to the reference time, and lacks duration adverbials or habitual markers required for DtUniv classification.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'I have given you authority' presents a completed transfer of authority as a single bounded occurrence with a construed endpoint, construing the act of giving as a transition event that is asserted as a completed whole.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have given (you) authority' is a present perfect form asserting a realized action in the actual world, not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.
TLTS_explanation: The target 'have given' is a simple present perfect (have + past participle) with no past auxiliary, future marking, modal auxiliary, or progressive aspect, so it does not instantiate TLTS.
VcPass_explanation: The construction 'have given you authority' is active voice (agent 'I' as subject, patient 'you' as object) lacking the required passive morphology of 'have been' + past participle with patient as subject.

FullText: » All things have been delivered to me from my Father . No one knows who the Son is except the Father . And no one knows who the Father is except the Son and those to whom the Son is willing to reveal him . «
ASTrns_explanation: The clause is morphologically passive ('have been delivered'), and the lexical verb 'deliver' licenses both an external argument (agent: 'my Father') and an internal argument (patient: 'All things'), satisfying the passive condition in set_to_1_if.
ASUncc_explanation: The target 'have been delivered' is a passive construction where the external argument (agent) is suppressed but still semantically present; the sole overt argument 'All things' functions as the theme/patient of an underlying transitive verb, not as an unaccusative internal argument.
ASUnrg_explanation: The verb 'have been delivered' is passive voice with an internal argument ('all things' as the logical object), which violates the requirement for ASUnrg that no internal argument be licensed.
AktAcc_explanation: The event 'have been delivered' denotes a punctual transfer of possession or authority from Father to Son, marking a single boundary transition rather than a durative process with an inherent culmination phase.
AktAch_explanation: The verb 'delivered' denotes a punctual transition into a new state (transfer of possession/control from Father to Son), can be modified by specific time-point adverbials, does not permit interruption, and lacks measurable internal duration, satisfying multiple set_to_1_if criteria.
AktAct_explanation: The verb 'have been delivered' denotes a completed transfer with a defined result state (things now possessed by the subject), not a dynamic durative activity without inherent endpoint.
AktSemel_explanation: The target 'have been delivered' entails a resulting change of state (things transitioning from not-delivered to delivered) and does not constitute a single punctual occurrence without consequent result, thus failing the semelfactive criteria.
AktState_explanation: The verb phrase 'have been delivered' denotes a bounded, completed dynamic event (transfer of things from Father to Son) with an inherent endpoint, not a state that holds over time without change.
ClCntfcl_explanation: The clause 'All things have been delivered to me from my Father' is a factual statement about a completed action in the actual world, not a counterfactual condition or unfulfilled wish, and contains no markers of contrary-to-fact meaning.
ClNegPol_explanation: The target gram 'have been delivered' is not negated; it presents the event as occurring in reality with no negation markers, negative quantifiers, or negative adverbs modifying it.
DtExp_explanation: The passive perfect 'have been delivered' introduces a resulting state (things are now in the possession/control of the subject) rather than focusing on the occurrence of the event as part of life history.
DtRes_explanation: The construction 'have been delivered' is a stative copular form describing an ongoing state of possession rather than encoding a transition; it reports that things are in a delivered state without indicating that a resulting state persists from a prior change of state at the reference time.
DtUniv_explanation: The clause 'All things have been delivered to me from my Father' describes a completed transfer of possession (punctual result) rather than a continuous state or activity extending to the reference time, lacking duration adverbials or non-terminating situation markers required for DtUniv.
TLAspPfv_explanation: The clause 'have been delivered' is a passive form asserting a bounded transition event (delivery as a completed whole) rather than an ongoing process, matching the set_to_1_if criterion for passive bounded transitions.
TLMdIrr_explanation: The gram 'have been delivered' is in the present perfect tense asserting a realized state of affairs in the actual world, not a projected, hypothetical, or non-actual situation.
